A considerable quantity of carpets and woollen stuffs is manufactured here; also chintzes, fire-arms, and yataghans; but black felt and opium constitute the principal articles of trade. The opium is obtained from the white poppy The annual produce of this article has been estimated been the Apameia of the Greeks and Romans. Pococke regards it as the Prynesia of Ptolemy. Its present name, signifying the 'Black castle of Opium,' distinguishes it from several other hisars in the neighbourhood,—such as Sevri-hisár, Bálá-hisár, Eski-karáhisár, Koch-hisár.
AFKA, a Mutuali v. of Syria, at the western foot of Libanus, 20 m. S by E of Tripoli. It is 3 hours distance from Lake Limun, and is supposed to be the ancient Aphasa.
AFLAHORE, a town in the Popo territory on the Slave-coast of Africa, 12 m. NW of Great Popo.

AFOGNAK, an island in the N Pacific ocean; in N lat. 58° 20', W long. 152° 30'. It lies to the N of Kadiack, and forms with that isle the E side of Chelikhoff strait. It is about 18 m. in circuit. Pop. 200. The Russians have an establishment upon this island.
AFRABA, a mountain of Abessinia, to the NW of Harrar, which has Mount Fresiz on the W, and Mount Asaboti on the NW, all inhabited by Isas.

AFRAGOLLA, a town of Naples, in the district of Casoria, 5 m. NNE of Naples. Pop. 3,000. Its chief manufacture is that of straw hats. Balbi in 1837 estimated the pop. at 13,000; but this must have been that of the entire commune.

AFRICA. Africa forms one of the great divisions of the world, and one the outlines of which are particularly welldefined. The African continent was known to the ancients; only a small extent of sea separates it from Europe; its coasts lie in sight of the earliest civilized countries; it was even the scene of some remarkable transactions 3,500 years ago, and the valley of the Nile was the cradle of commerce, of art, and science; yet it is certain that little was ever known of its interior, and it is very doubtful whether the ancients had any knowledge of its southern extremity. Nor are they much surpassed by the moderns in their acquaintance with this mysterious region. Our ships have circumnavigated the coasts of A. for more than three centuries, and yet, with a few exceptions, our knowledge of these districts is very incomplete; while the interior still presents to the eye of Science a blank in geography,—an unsolved problem in moral as well as physical science. In 1838, the president of our Royal Geographical society thus summed up the actual amount of our knowledge of A .: "The immediate neighbourhood of our own colony of the cape of Good Hope, -some of the more accessible parts of the coast of Barbary,-a few points on the western coast of the Red sea, -some tracts of the adjoining Desert,-portions of the English and French possessions on the Gambia and Senegal,—the banks of the Nile below the first or second cataract, -Denham's and Clapperton's routes to the lake Chad,—that of Park to Sego,—and a glimpse of the lately discovered banks of the Quorra and the Chadda,-comprise nearly all we may be said (even imperfectly) to know throughout this portion of the globe. [Geog. Journ.] The last 12 years have however added somewhat to our knowledge. Commercial enterprise has contributed more perhaps than any other cause to lay open to human curiosity the remote regions of the globe; but the rivers of A., although many of them are large and beneficent, have, until a very recent period, afforded no certain inlet to its central regions, and the trade of Europeans has been confined to narrow districts along the coast. Zeal for the propagation of religious opinions has in many instances proved an extensive cause of geographical discovery; yet missionary labours have encountered great barriers to exploring in Africa; still, both in Eastern and Southern Africa, the two most important discoveries of this century the snowy mountain of Kilimanjaro, and the Great Lake of S Africa—have been made by the zeal and energy of missionaries. The march of Conquest herself has been arrested or turned aside by the fiery deserts which extend from Egypt to the Atlantic; and Science, even while stimulated by baffled curiosity, has failed to surmount the moral and physical barriers which here impede her progress. The astronomer and mathematician encounter a formidable and often insurmountable obstacle to their labours of observation in the superstitious ignorance of the natives, who regard their scientific operations as so many magical incantations; and there is something even in the quality of the African atmosphere which is opposed to their labours. Captain Roussin, who was employed on an expedition of observation to the western coast of A., complained of a certain dimness or haziness in the northern part of the tropic of Cancer which repeatedly interrupted his measurements. This haze or obscurity seems to be occasioned by the quantity of finely pulverized sand which the wind raises into the atmosphere in its passage across the wide deserts of this continent. It prevails throughout the greater part of the year; and during its continuance, no star which is not elevated 30° above the horizon is visible to the eye. In the southern districts the position of the sun likewise renders astronomical observations extremely difficult; and the heat causes so great a refraction, that the real position of the heavenly bodies is frequently miscalculated,especially when the observer is operating with instruments which are liable to be affected by heat. Topographical mistakes have likewise been abundantly committed by African travellers through their ignorance of the languages spoken by the natives. Thus we were at one time gravely informed of the existence of a kingdom called Kassentai, in Guinea, which was forthwith identified with Ashantee by some of our African geographers. But it afterwards appeared that this word kassentai means nothing more than, 'I do not understand you,' which had been the simple answer given by some Negroes to a European querist. We also apprehend that modern geographers and travellers have borrowed too slavishly from their predecessors in all that respects African geography. Thus Jackson owes more to Chenier than one might suspect from the manner in which he affects to talk of him. Meredith's treatise on the coast of Africa is in great part a mere compilation from Isert and Bosman. Malte Brun disappoints the reader in every page of his African geography. Ali Bey himself-who was certainly not in want of opportunities for personal observation, and who is so much lauded by Burckhardt-repeatedly betrays some ignorance of his subject. In general, the information furnished by natives respecting the geography of A. has proved exceedingly fallacious. One principal source of error in their case has evidently been the different names applied by Negroes and by Arabs, and even by tribes of the same nation, to the same object, town, or district of country. Thus we find the same country indicated by the very dissimilar names of Dar Saleg, Mobba, Wara, and Waday. Thus too, we observe a town, which, when first visited by Mungo Park, was called Kanipe, receiving no other name from its own inhabitants, when he visited it a second time, than Sisekando. In enumerating the various sources of error and ignorance with respect to African geography, we must not omit to observe that this portion of our globe has also undergone considerable physical as well as moral revolutions. Wherever water flows, vegetation will flourish and spread itself; but in many districts of A. the springs which once fertilized the soil no longer flow, and with them every trace of vegetable life also has disappeared. Many large rivers which seem in some period of remote antiquity to have received powerful tributaries from the northern countries of A. are now dried up; while others, both in the north and south, are no longer supplied with a sufficient body of water to enable them to roll their way through thirsty deserts, with undiminished flood, to the ocean. In some cases the barren sand seems to be obliterating the ancient features of this continent, and threatens ultimately to reduce those portions to a sterile desert. Name.] A. was called by the Greeks Libya; to the Romans it was known by its present name. A small province in the northern part—in more ancient times a Carthaginian district—to which the Romans applied the term Africa Propria, seems to have imparted its own name to the whole continent; in like manner as the name Asia, originally applied to the peninsula of which Caria formed the western extremity, came to be gradually extended to the whole of that continent. Bochart derives the word Africa from feric a Punic word, signifying 'an ear of corn,' with a supposed reference to the fertility of those parts of this continent which were known to the Phœnicians. Others discover the derivation of the present appellation in the Latin apricus, sunny, -or the Greek privative a, and peinn, 'cold,' which may signify a hot clime, or a clime without cold. A more natural etymology has been suggested in the word Afrygah, which, in the language of Carthage, would signify a colony, which that African state was of Tyre. The term Libya—which most commonly denoted exclusively the maritime district between the Greater Syrtis and Egypt, but was sometimes used in the same extensive application as the term Africa-is derived by Varro from Libs, the Greek name of the south wind; by many it is supposed to be of Hebrew origin, and to have been derived from the Lubim of the Old Testament; while Dr. Hyde, deducing it from an Arabic word denoting 'resplendency,' sees in it an allusion to the well known phenomenon in A., the mirage! Extent.] In point of superficial extent, A. holds the next rank to America and Asia. It presents the appearance of a vast peninsula united to the adjacent Asiatic continent, at its north-east extremity, between the Red sea and the Mediterranean, by the isthmus of Suez, a strip of land about 75 m. in breadth. Its general outline is that of an irregular triangle, of which the southern extremity forms the blunt apex. It extends from Cape Blanco or Ras-el-Abiad, in the Mediterranean, in N lat. 37° 20', to Cape das Agulhas, in S lat. 34° 51'; thus measuring more than 72 degrees of latitude from N to S, or 4,330 geographical, or 4,990 English m. Its breadth at the equator is 2,040 geographical, or 2,360 English m.; but from Cape Guardafui, in N lat. 11° 50', E long. 51° 21', to Cape Verde, in N lat. 14° 43', W long. 17° 34', its breadth is nearly equal to its length. From the Mediterranean, where its breadth is already considerable, it becomes gradually broader until within a few degrees of the equator; it then contracts suddenly, and, passing southwards, narrows by degrees until it terminates at the cape of Good Hope. - The superficial extent of this vast region can of course be only approximately estimated. Malte Brun, and the editors of the 'Dictionnaire Geographique Universel,' estimate it at 1,750,000 French sq. leagues of 25 to a degree, or 13,430,000 British sq. m.; and Golberry at 1,600,000 of these leagues. Stein has calculated it at 511,803 German sq. m., exclusive of the islands; which is less than either Ukert's admeasurement or Gräberg's. We subjoin the elements of the approximative calculations of the two latter geographers in German sq. m .:- According According to Ukert. to Graberg. Thus, according to Ukert, the whole region of A. has a superficial extent of 531,638 German, or 11,961,675 English sq. m.; but, without the large islands, admeasures 521,106 German, or 11,724,885 English sq. Gräberg's calculation corresponds pretty closely m. with this. There is, however, a very considerable culations. British geographers have usually assigned 11,500,000 sq. m. to A., inclusive of its islands. Coasts and Boundaries.] The African continent presents throughout its whole extent of 16,000 m. of coast-line a very regular contour, unbroken by any large peninsulas, bays, or gulfs, and clear of islands. From the cape of Good Hope to the isthmus of Suez, the shores of the African continent are washed by the Indian ocean, which, betwixt the mainland and the large island of Madagascar, forms the channel of Mozambique. An arm of this ocean forms the Arabian gulf, or Red sea, which separates A. from Asia. The Atlantic ocean bathes the southern and western shores, separating it, at several thousand miles distance, from the coast of America. From the western extremity of the cape of Good Hope to the gulf of Guinea, in 5° N. lat., the coast forms a pretty regular line, inclining gently westwards; but at the latter parallel, the coast-line strikes out suddenly and more rapidly towards the west. Having reached, in Cape Verde, its extreme western point, it turns north-east, and pursues this direction to the straits of Gibraltar. The A. coast-line bounded by the Mediterranean, and running from W to E, forms innumerable sinuosities, of which the most remarkable are the gulf of Cabes, the gulf of Syrtis or Sidra, and the gulf of Alexandria. The immense maritime cincture we have now traced, almost isolates A. from the rest of the world: in two points only is it nearly interrupted, -namely, at the straits of Bab-el-Mandeb, or the entrance to the Arabian gulf, which are 16 m. in width, and at the straits of Gibraltar forming the entrance to the Mediterranean, which do not exceed 10 m. in width. But the only point at which a junction with any other part of the world is actually effected, is at the low sandy isthmus of Suez, betwixt the head of the Arabian gulf and the SE extremity of the Mediterranean.—The harbours and roadsteds of this continent are generally uninviting to the mariner; and no gulf or inland sea opens a way to him into the interior. There are of course some exceptions—but they are very few indeed-to this observation; and, generally speaking, the whole continent is singularly devoid of harbours, or even of deep gulfs or bays, or of large rivers, the want of which is a barrier to exploring, and will in some measure account for our limited knowledge of its interior. The gulfs of Guinea and Syrtis are alike shunned by the navigator: and although the coasts of Guinea and Senegal present several mouths of rivers accompanied with islands, yet sand - bars universally obstruct their mouths, and many formidable obstacles oppose themselves to their free navigation for any distance from the coast. The coast washed by the Indian ocean lies low, like the opposite shores of Guinea; but here coast is equally inhospitable for shipping [Howison]. Capes and Gulfs.] We shall now conduct the reader once more round the coasts of A., and point out to him the capes and promontories which we meet with from the shores of the Mediterranean to the Indian ocean. Proceeding along the northern coast, we come first to Cape Bon, on the NE coast of the kingdom of Tunis; next to Cape Spartel, on the W shore of the straits of Gibraltar; then sailing southward, we discover, in the Atlantic, Cape Geer, or Ghir, on the SW boundary of Marocco, from which commences difference in some of the elements of the above cal- the extensive range of Atlas; Cape Bojador, first doubled by the Portuguese in 1433; Cape Blanco; | 10° and 20° S. The edges of the tableau are Cape Verd, E of the islands of that name, the most westerly point of the continent; Cape Mesurado, at the mouth of the Mesurado river; Cape Palmas on the W, and the cape of the Three Points on the S of the coast of Guinea; Cape Negro on the NW, and Cape das Voltas on the SW of Caffraria; the Cape of Good Hope, the SW promontory of Africa; after doubling which we come to Cape das Agulhas, in the Indian sea; Cape Corrientes near the tropic of Capricorn; Cape Delgado N of the Comoro isles; Ras Hafun, the eastern, and Ras Asser or Cape Guardafui, the north-eastern extremity of Africa, and the entrance of the gulf of Aden.—Its gulfs are those of Sidra, Kabes, and Tunis, in the Mediterranean; the gulf N of the mouth of the Gambia; the gulf of Guinea, S of the Gold coast, by far the most considerable; and the gulf of Sofala, near the southern entrance of the Mozambique channel. Physical features.] A. is certainly the most compact of the great divisions of the globe; appearingto use Ritter's expression-"like a trunk without limbs." Hence the periphery of the African continent is, in comparison with the superficial extent of the whole, much less than that of Asia, America, or Europe. These latter continents have their coasts elongated by being deeply indented with bays, capes, and necks of land; but this, we have already seen, is not the case with A. There are only a very few islands near the shores of this continent; and, with the exception perhaps of Madagascar, none of them seem to be connected in physical structure with the adjacent continent, although this is the case with almost all islands in other quarters of the world. The equator cuts Africa into two masses of unequal magnitude. The northern portion has fully twice the superficial area of the southern, and may be considered as about equal in extent to South America; the southern portion is about the size of New Holland. General configuration.] Africa may be viewed in three great divisions: 1. The table-lands, extending from the Cape colony to Nubia and the southern confines of the Sahara. 2. The lowlands, comprising the Sahara. 3. The system of the Atlas mountains and other smaller groups between the Sahara and the Mediterranean, forming a connecting link, in physical features generally, with Europe, which it approaches in this respect nearer than to the rest of Africa. The highlands and mountains comprise about double the area of that of the lowlands, or at least 7,000,000 sq. m. The great African table-land, analogous to the entire continent, forms a very compact mass, its edges being generally parallel to the coasts, at a distance of 100 to 300 m. It is probable that this immense mass of elevated country is nowhere interrupted by any extensive valley or deep cut, and the depression formed in the N by the Nile is perhaps the most considerable one, splitting the table-land into two branches, the eastern one of which continues to Abessinia, whence, in about 14° and 15° N lat., it descends abruptly towards the Red sea and the Nubian plains from an average height of 8,000 ft. The western branch stretches towards Lake Chad, where it terminates with the Mendefi mountains, probably 8,000 ft. high, and at the river Kawara or Niger with the King William mountains, elevated about 3,000 ft.; beyond this river the table-land may be said to extend to the Kong mountains, reaching to the head sources of the river Senegal. This tableland is the most extensive of the globe in area, and probably also the most perfect, presenting comparatively few mountains rising out of it,-only two ranges, there is reason to believe, being considerable in height and extent, the one in the northern half, near the equator, the other between the parallels of I the N, where the river rises, wear clothes. All these often, but not always, formed by terraces, sometimes by one single more or less steep ascent, as the eastern edge of the Abessinian plateau; sometimes these edges are also formed by barrier-like mountainranges, as the Roggeveld, Nieuweveld, Winter, and Snieuw mountains, in the S. Frequently there stand out on these extremities of the elevated plain, sentinel-like isolated summits of great height, as the Abba Jared, rising 15,200 ft., on or near the NE edge; in the S the Spitzkop or Compass Berg, with a height of 10,250 ft.; while, in the former instance, no such height is known to occur again for a distance of 400 to 500 m. to the S, and in the latter instance, even for 900 m. to 1,000 m. further inland. On the eastern and western edge we know likewise two very conspicuous mountains; both are in the northern portion, the western one being the Cameroons, terminating close to the sea in Biafra bay, and attaining in the highest summit, Mongo-ma-Lobah peak, the height of 13,760 ft.; the eastern one, the recently discovered Kilimanjaro, covered with perpetual snow, and most probably not under 20,000 ft. high; it is situated in about 4° S lat., and 36° E long., 200 m. W of Mombas, and was first seen in 1848 by the Rev. Mr. Rebman. The northern range of mountains, rising from the table-land under the name of Mountains of the Moon, have been reported to exist 1,700 years ago; all travellers in Central Africa hear of them; and yet to this day we know little more of them than Ptolemy himself. These mountains have been placed, in the shape of a long range from W to E across the continent of Africa, in various latitudes; it is more probable, however, that they have an entirely different direction, namely from N to S, and that they form the southern continuation of the Abessinian highlands, and the water-parting between the rivers of the Indian ocean and the upper branches of the Nile. When Mr. Rebman penetrated from Mombas as far as Djagga, a mountainous country situated about 200 m. from the coast, and discovered Mount Kilimanjaro, he ascertained also that beyond this mountain is a country named Uniamézi. This word, according to Dr. Krapf, the missionary, means the 'Country of the moon,' and is no doubt identical with Mono-moézi, a name which has been known to us since the time of the Portuguese settlements in S Africa. There is also ground enough to identify this name with Ptolemy's Mountains of the Moon, which, according to that writer, formed a maritime range of great length, lying to the W of the low districts along the sea-coast opposite to the island of Menuthias (Zanzibar). The position and direction here assigned to the Mountains of the Moon, rest on the results of the newest discoveries in Eastern Africa, as well as on the opinion of the most able African geographers, among which the names of Beke and Russegger stand pre-eminent. A second interior mountain-system, in the southern half of the table-land, capped with snow, is likewise confirmed by recent discoveries. According to Cavazzi, the south-eastern boundaries of Benguela, in about 15° S lat., are formed by a lofty range of cold and of snowy mountains. The recently discovered Lake Ngami, in about 20° S lat., and 24° E long., receives large rivers from the N, which must be considered to rise in snowy mountains, for even after forming the lake, and issuing from it, and flowing at least 300 m., its waters are clear as crystal, soft, and cold, imparting the idea of melted snow, which is confirmed by the rise of 3 ft. in July and August. The natives were ignorant of the cause of this, but said it was not caused by rain. They also reported that the natives in the mountainous country far to circumstances go to confirm the early Portuguese accounts of a high mountain-group N of the parallel of 20°. How far these mountains may extend towards the equator is at present impossible to determine, but from Mr. Cooley's valuable labours relating to the interior of Africa between 5° and 12° S lat., we learn that this vast region apparently does not contain any very extensive or elevated mountain-system. It is to be hoped that the middle of the 19th cent. will at last lift the veil which has hitherto covered this great elevated interior of Africa; and no previous period in the history of African inland discovery could have justified more sanguine expectations than the present moment, when this terra incognita is being explored from the N, S, and E. In the N there is the expedition of Messrs. Richardson, Barth, and Overweg, principally fitted out by the English government, but with the aid of the Berlin Geographical society to the two Prussian members. They are to proceed from Tripoli to Lake Chad, explore it and the surrounding countries, and thence shape their route in a south-easterly direction towards the shores of the Indian ocean, to reach, if possible, Mombas. From recent letters we learn that they left Tripoli for the interior on the 29th of March, proceeding by way of Murzuk and Kano, the safest route at present to Lake Chad. Important results may be expected from this expedition. On the eastern coast, at Rabbai Empia, near Mombas, are stationed the zealous and enterprising missionaries, Krapf and Rebman, the latter of which has already so successfully terminated two journeys to the interior, and who will lose no opportunities to penetrate farther. Lastly, in the S, to follow up Livingston's discovery of the great Lake Ngami, Messrs. Galton and Anderson have sailed for the Cape of Good Hope, intending to start from Algoa bay, via Kolesberg, Lataku, and Kolobeng, to the lake. The party is provided with the necessary instruments for scientific observations, and also with three boats, constructed upon the most improved principles, in order to enable them to navigate the lake and its tributaries, after which they will proceed northwards by one of the large rivers reported to empty themselves into the lake. This latter expedition, although important enough in itself, is less so than those in the N, while at the same time it is much more certain of success and of early results. Let us hope that these various explorers may be spared to carry out their plans for the sake of science and civilization. [Petermann.] The Great African plain and desert.] To the N of the high table-land of Southern Africa, and of the Kong mountains, an immense plain stretches northwards to the foot of the Atlas, presenting two very different regions—the one fertile called Sudan, the other the barren Sahara. The wide expansion of sandy desert almost everywhere to be met with throughout this continent, forms a prominent feature in the physical geography of A. These wastes, in this division of the globe, are not confined as with us to the extent of a few miles surrounded by regions of greater fertility; they are extensive sand-oceans containing only a few scattered isle-like spots fit for human habitation. Destitute of moisture, the sand of which they are composed is borne about on every blast, or carried forward with an undulating motion having no distant resemblance to the waves of the sea, but more destructive to the helpless traveller who is overtaken by it. The largest of these extraordinary regions is that called by way of eminence the Sahara, or Desert, which is upwards of 2,600 m. in length, and 750 m. in breadth, running almost across the whole breadth of Northern A., between the parallels of 15° and 59° N. lat. There are in the Sahara, particularly in the eastern part of it, a few fertile spots of scanty verdure; but it is in general desolate beyond the wildest wastes of European land. "No cooling breezes"-to quote the words of two recent travellers-"here freshen the air,-the sun descends in overpowering force,—the winds scorch as they pass, and bring with them billows of sand, rolling along in masses frightfully suffocating, which sometimes swallow up whole caravans and armies, burying them in their pathless depths," Vegetative steppes encircle the Sahara; those towards the south being watered by the Niger, and intersected by the vanguards of the high lands or great plateau already described; and those on the north, or the steppes of Biledulgerid, being intersected by the ridges of the Atlas. It is divided into two parts by a tract of stony country which, between 13° and 15° E long., traverses it from S to N.—The region called Sudan extends from the eastern declivity of the Kong mountains to the Bahr-el-Abiad or western branch of the Nile. The Atlas chain.] The configuration of all the known chains of Africa supports the general view of its surface, as exhibiting a succession of elevated table-lands gradually rising above each other as their distance from the coast increases. The chain of mountains which lines nearly the whole of the north coast of A., consists of a series of five or six small chains, rising one behind another in this terrace-like form, throughout a large portion of Barbary. This chain—which ancient fable loved to represent as supporting the heavens—is known to European geographers under the general appellation of the Atlas range. It stretches between 28° and 37° N lat., commencing at the bottom of the gulf of the Great Syrtis, and running N to Cape Blanco. The plateau which forms the desert of Barca, separates the Atlas system from the mountains of Egypt; but some geographers insist on prolonging the appellation of Atlas to Mount Tchefti, and the mountains of Gerboda and Harutch, lying farther eastwards. The Atlas chain attains its greatest elevation between Fez and Marocco. That part of the range which overlooks the plains of Marocco has been stated to attain, on the summit called Miltsin, in N lat. 31° 12', the altitude of 11,400 ft, above the sea; but this elevation must be exceeded by the mountains of the W of Abyssinia, which, according to Salt, have their summits covered with perpetual snow, under a burning sun, and in the torrid zone. The Atlas—as we have already stated—does not form a cordillera, or a continuous chain of mauntains protracted in a single line; it is composed of several chains, and some isolated groups, with a general formation inclining more towards sharp ridges and points than to Alpine peaks [Washington]. These unequal masses, differing in form and direction, are separated in some instances by fruitful plains abounding in cereal products; in others by sterile deserts, whose surface is covered with burning sands, or sprinkled with rugged fragments of black rock interspersed with patches of salt; and in others by green and lovely valleys watered by limpid streams, and fruitful in olives, oranges, and pomegranates. The Arabian geographers considered this tract as more European than African in its character,—an opinion which the physical features of the opposite Spanish peninsula, with its Sierra Nevada, fully justify. The different portions of the Atlas have received general denominations. The Great Atlas is the range in which the highest summits occur, extending from the bottom of the gulf of Kabes to Cape Gir. The section of the Great Atlas, comprised betwixt Fez and Marocco, where the chain attains its greatest elevation, is called the High Atlas. To the eastwards, the Great Atlas joins itself to the mountains of Gadamis and Harudj-el-Akouad, which surround the Great Syrtis. The Little Atlas is a chain of mountains which approaches nearer to the coast, and runs sometimes parallel to the Great Atlas. This chain commences at Tangiers, and running westwards is prolonged, by the heights of Uselet, to Cape Bon, the Promontorium Mercurii of the ancients. It then bends around the gulf of Kabes, and the Great Syrtis, in the Ghurian and Terhounan hills. The Little Atlas is united to the Great Atlas by several transverse chains, of which the most elevated are the Jurjuras to the east of Algiers, and the Errifs between Fez and Tangiers. All the chains which unite the Great and the Little Atlas, as well as those which run out from the southern flanks of the former and sink into the Desert, form different basins, from which flow short unnavigable streams which precipitate themselves on the W into the Atlantic, and on the N into the Mediterranean; and on the S are lost in lakes or evaporated in the sands of the desert. Egyptian mountains, At some distance to the east of the Harudj mountains, and in the same line, are the mountains of Magarah and Mokattam, situated at the northern extremity of the Libyan chain which runs along the western bank of the Nile as far as the cataracts. The country between the Nile and the Red sea is covered with mountains, and declines gradually from Abyssinia towards Egypt. In the neighbourhood of Cairo, a low branch runs off ENE towards the isthmus of Suez; and on the W of the Delta, the Gerdobah mountains gradually rise towards the high table-land of Barca. Abessinian mountains.] Abessinia is an elevated table-land, overlooked by a loftier range of high lands running from S to N, and intersected on the W by several chains known to us only by report, in which are the sources of several streams, probably affluents of the Bako, Sobat or Telfi, and other tributaries of the Bahr-el-Abiad, or White Nile. The hills between the Sahara and the upper part of the valley of the Nile do not appear to attain a great elevation; but those on the E of the Nile are considerably elevated. The lateral chain of the Red sea, the Troglodytic chain, resembles the Atlas in its calcareous steeps. Rivers. A. is less favoured than either Europe, Asia, or America, with rivers, It is not wonderful that, under a tropical sun, and amid thirsty soil, many African streams should disappear during summer; but a mystery altogether impenetrable still hangs over several of the great rivers of this continent. The outlet of the Niger or Joliba was unknown until the Landers' late expedition; and the outlets of the Bornu, the Kullah, the Misselad in Nigritia, and the Jedi or Al-Jedi in the Zab country, are still unknown to us. Rivers of Northern Africa.] The Nile is the only river of Africa which flows into the Mediterranean. It is also the largest river of this continent, having a course of probably not less than 3,600 m.; from its mouth to Khartum 1,780 Eng. m.; from Khartum to farthest point explored 1,300 m.; thence to source probably 500 m.; total 3,580 Eng. m., or second only to the Mississippi-Missouri. The total length to the source of Bahr-el-Azrek 2,830 Eng. m., and the area of its basin about 525,000 sq. m.; the basin of Bahrel-Abiad about 1,000,000 sq. m.; area of the entire basin of the Nile 1,525,000 Eng. sq. m.—The streams of the Atlas regions which enter the Atlantic, though numerous, are inconsiderable as to volume of water. South of this region, the arid character of the Sahara is found extending even to the shores of the ocean, and hardly disappearing before we arrive at the Senegal. In fact the drifting sands of the Great desert seem to be here fast gaining upon the ocean. Rivers of Western Africa.] The Senegal river has its source in the high lands of the Mandingo terrace, between the sources of the Niger on the E, and those of the Gambia on the W. Mollien-a young Frenchman, who explored the sources of the Senegal and Gambia in 1818—differs in several particulars of his report regarding these rivers from that of Mungo Park. Mollien affirms that the Senegal rises in the Kong mountains, and flows westwards along the southern limits of the Sahara, where it receives the Kokoro and the Falamah, and forms the cataracts of Gurma and Felu. Beyond these falls, he represents it as assuming the appearance of a placid and majestic stream, rolling its mighty flood with a motion scarcely perceptible through a vast plain; it then turns NW, and divides itself into two branches, after the reunion of which it flows westwards, and discharges itself, by one broad mouth, into the Atlantic, in about 16° 5' N lat. The Senegal, like the Nile, takes its rise at a great distance from the coast. Both rivers divide themselves into branches near their embouchure, and form islands which are more fertile than any part of their banks; and this fertility is the result in both of periodical overflowings, during which a quantity of fresh vegetable earth is always deposited on the flooded surface of the islands. In Egypt, however, the N and NE winds being cooled in their passage across the sea and over the snows of Lebanon, preserve a moderate temperature in the districts lying along the river, except during a few weeks of summer; but the country through which the Senegal flows is exposed to the almost constant action of an E wind, which, blowing across the burning surface of the Sáhara, attains to such a temperature as renders the atmosphere scarcely respirable. The ascent of the Senegal is opposed both by wind and current, while that of the Nile is aided by the N wind, which carries vessels swiftly up to Thebes; and the descent of the Egyptian river is rapidly accomplished by the aid of the current. At the mouth of the Senegal there is a shifting bar which prevents the entrance of vessels drawing more than 10 ft. of water. The Felu rock also stops the upper navigation of the river for at least seven months of the year; during the remaining five the river, being flooded, obliterates this barrier. The total course of the Senegal is above 800 m. It was long confounded with the Niger. M. Mollien discovered the sources of the Gambia a little to the southward of those of the Rio-Grande, in 10° 36' N lat. It rises in a valley nearly enclosed by barren mountains. After running a few leagues eastwards, it turns N, and preserves that direction to 13° 22' N. lat., where it changes its direction, and runs S to the parallel of 11° 18', and to within 17 leagues of its source. It then takes a general NW direction to the latitude of 14° 30'; and finally, after a circuitous route, discharges itself into the Atlantic, between Cape St. Mary and the isle of Sangomar. This river receives a number of tributary streams; and at its embouchure is about 20 m. broad. It contracts to one league in the neighbourhood of Albreda, and preserves this latter breadth as far as the falls of Barraconda, or to about 120 leagues from its mouth, to which distance it is navigable by vessels of 150 tons; for nearly one-half of that distance it is navigable by ships of war carrying 40 guns. At Barraconda the bed of the river is interrupted by a range of rocks which runs across it; above this barrier the river winds through a flat country. Like the Nile and Senegal, the Gambia is subject to annual inundations; but while the Senegal is only fully navigable in the rainy season, the Gambia cannot be navigated except in the dry season, for while the rains give it an enormous increase of depth, the inordinate weight and rapidity of the current becomes so great that no vessels can stem it. The course of this river has been estimated at 610 miles.—The Rio Grande, the Rio Mesurado, the Rio Volta, and some on the coast of Guinea. The Niger or Quorra.] Until the expedition of the Landers, in 1830, there was not a more difficult problem in geography than to determine the course of the Niger. Many attempts had been made to do so, both in ancient as well as modern times, but all had only left us involved in the darkness of conflicting theories and contradictory reports. The very direction of this river was for a long period a debatable question. The Arabs of the middle ages attributed to the Niger a westward course to 'the Sea of Darkness,' or the Atlantic; they also conceived that the Niger and the Nile sprung from the same origin. Edrisi, Abulfeda, and Leo Africanus, asserted that the Niger flowed from E to W. Leo Africanus, however, acknowledges that some geographers held it to run from W to E. This was in fact the opinion of Herodotus 2,000 years before; and in this opinion Ptolemy coincided. The Portuguese, seeing the Senegal, the Gambia, and other great rivers proceeding from the unknown interior of Africa, discharge themselves into the Atlantic, conceived that these rivers might be the mouths of the Niger, and therefore gave it a westward course; and in this opinion they were followed by Delisle and Bruce. It was reserved for our countryman, Park, to decide the question as to the direction of the Niger in favour of the old Grecian geographer. On the 21st of July, 1796, that intrepid traveller beheld, from the heights of Sego, "the majestic Niger flowing slowly from west to east." Equally unsettled were the theories as to the source of this river: for whilst some believed it to originate in the mountains of Mauritania, others affirmed that it issued from a lake to the south of Bornu; and others, as we have hinted, identified its fountain-head with that of the Nile. It was decided by Major Laing, from observation, that the great central river of Africa has its source near Mount Loma, in the country of the Sulimas, on the northern declivities of the Kong mountains, between 9° and 10° W long., and at an elevation of 1,638 feet above the level of the Atlantic. But though the source and direction of the Niger were thus determined, a problem equally difficult of solution remained, namely, where was its embouchure! One, notwithstanding the moderate elevation of its source, supposed it to join the Nile of Egypt; another conducted it to the lake of Wangara, which he made its termination; whilst another improved as he thought on the idea by conducting it through that extensive swamp, to the Bahr-al-Ghazelle, or 'River of antelopes,' and thence through the sands of Bilma to the Mediterranean. A fourth conjecture traced it to the Atlantic, under the name of the Zaire; whilst a fifth, started by Reichard in 1803, and supported with great ingenuity and research by Mr. M'Queen, conducted it to the bight of Benin. This last conjecture is now verified. The two brothers, Landers-one of whom had been Captain Clapperton's servant, and accompanied him in that capacity to Sokatu, and had there performed the last sad duties to his deceased master -undertook to explore the course of this mysterious stream from Boussa, to which Park had traced it. On the 31st of March, 1830, they commenced their over-land journey from Badagry; and on the 20th of September, they left Boussa and proceeded down the river, there running due S. At Rabba, they found it running SE to Kakunda in 8° 30' E long., and 8° 30' N lat. From Kakunda it runs due S to its junction with the Chadda. After the confluence of these two streams, the river varies from 2 to 5 and 6 m. wide during the periodical inundations, and takes a SW course through the mountains. At Kirree, the Niger now called the Quorra sends off a branch for the first time, so that Kirree may be regarded as the apex or other rivers have their embouchures further to the S, | head of its delta; the uppermost branch goes SW to Benin, and is the Rio Formoso or most western mouth of the river. The main stream runs further S to Ibú, where it expands into a lake, from which it issues in three branches, the middle branch, or Rio Nun, being the main stream, and entering the sea at Cape Formoso. In its further course downwards, the Nun sends off four other branches. The whole course of the Niger is about 1,660 Eng. m.; area of basin 800,000 sq. m. Its delta occupies a base of about 250 m. in extent. By the discovery of the course and termination of the long-sought Niger, a way is probably now opened up into the interior of Africa. By means of steam-vessels, the various branches and main stream of the Quorra will be ascended with ease; and in the season of the periodical swell, they may ascend to Timbuktu, and carry thither the productions and the civilization of Europe. Rivers of Southern Africa.] The great African river S of the equator is the Zaire or Congo, which flows into the Atlantic in 61° S lat. Captain Tuckey ascertained the mouth of this river to be about 3 m. in breadth, with a main depth of 40 fathoms, and an average flow of about 41 m. an hour. It rises in the interior of the country, and flows through Angola. Between the Congo and Cape Negro are numerous streams, of which the principal is the Coanga. From Cape Negro to the Orange river we have a coast of 800 or 900 m. almost without fresh water. Orange river descends from the Snow mountains. It may be said to commence at Campbell's Dorp, 600 m. directly E from its mouth, where two rivers of the same name, but distinguished by the initials nu and ky, the Nu- Gareep, and Ky-Gareep, i. e., 'the Black and the Yellow Gareep,'-unite, and flow into a basin, formed on one side by the chain of the mountains of Kaop, on the other by the Kong mountains and those of Camhanni which Burchell passed over. Area of basin about 360,000 sq. m. Of this river it would appear that though it reaches the ocean during the wet season, in the dry season its communication with the sea is interrupted, and its channel presents only a series of lagunes and pools.—Nearly all the rivulets and mountain-torrents in South A. cease to flow from December and January till May, and several of the most considerable rivers are without current during that season. Rivers of Eastern Africa.] The Quilimane, from which proceeds the Zambese, or Zambezi, is the chief river on the eastern side of Africa. Access to the Quilimane is rendered difficult to vessels of considerable draught by a bar which extends across its entrance, and on which the sea often breaks with great violence. The tides also run with considerable velocity here; and when the wind is violent, and in opposition to the stream of the river, the conflict between the two elements is often terrific. It has been suggested that the Zambese, and the Zaire on the opposite coast, may very nearly complete the insulation of the southern part of the continent.-Farther N on this coast we find the outlet of streams from the interior; but there is not a stream of any note from Patta, in 2° 15' S. lat., to the isthmus of Suez. Lakes.] The African continent—so far as we yet know-possesses only three large lakes, L. Chad, L. Nyassi, L. Ngami; probably others may be discovered. The Chad is a vast expanse of fresh water, in about the 13th parallel of N lat., receives two rivers, the Yeou from the W, and the Shary from the S. The Chad recedes and advances with the rainy season; it abounds with fish, and the country around it is clothed with all the splendour of tropical vegetation. Major Denham was obliged to leave 140 m. of the E side of this lake unexplored; consequently, it remains uncertain what rivers flow into it or issue from it in that quarter.-There is a lake to the W of the Chad, and nearly in the same latitude, near Timbuktu, called Debo, or Dibbie, through which the Joliba flows; and some travellers speak of a lake called Non, and of another called Fitre, as existing in this quarter .- On the boundaries of Tunis and Algiers the lake Lodejah occurs. In Tunis is the lake of Benzerta, the ancient Hipponitus lacus; and the lake of Jebel Ishkel, the ancient Sisaræ palus. Neither of these lakes exceeds 8 m. in length.—In Egypt there are the lake of Kerun and the Natron lakes; and in Abessinia is the lake of Dembea or Tzana, which is intersected by the Bahr-el-Azrek, or eastern branch of the Nile. On the great African table-land many lakes are reported to exist, but the positions of two only are tolerably known. The Lake Nyassi, or 'the sea,' is a narrow sheet of water, about 400 geog. m. long, by 30 m. wide, lying in a NW and SE direction, between the parallels of 8° and 12° S lat., and 28° and 33° E long., and covering an area of 12,000 sq. g. m., or one-half the extent of Ireland. This lake is said to be studded with islands. It is variously known by the names of Ziwa, Nhanja, Murusura, Maravi, Zambezi, and Zambre. Maravi is probably a name for boats or vessels, and Zambezi is the name of a river flowing into the Indian ocean near Kilimani, a branch of which rises near the SE end of the lake, but does not flow from it; nor is this lake known to have any outlet. [Lacerda; Cooley.] Recent intelligence from Dr. Krapf reports another lake W of Mombás, beyond Kilimanjaro, in the Uniamesi country. The second great lake S of the equator is that recently discovered by the Rev. Mr. Livingston, called by the natives N'gama. Starting from Koloberg, a station in 241° S lat., and 26° E long., and travelling 300 m. across the desert of Bakalihari, the party came upon the fine river Zuga, 30 yards wide, issuing from the lake. The NE extremity of the lake, where is a station of the Batasana tribe, is placed in 20° 20' S lat., and 24° E long. The extent of the lake is unknown, but the natives say it requires 25 days to travel round it. Its height, by thermometer, is stated to be 2,800 ft. above the sea. [Petermann.] Climate.] If the climate of America is distinguished by superabundant moisture and cold, that of A. is not less remarkable for its general want of humidity, and its warmth: of this fact, the immense extent of arid and burning deserts affords incontrovertible proof. The most northern and the most southern districts are equally without a winter. The sun, when vertical, ison . always brings with him a train of clouds, which pour down upon the subjacent country an incessant deluge. When the sun is in the N, the rainy season begins in the countries lying northwards from the equator; when in the S, the rainy season is to the south of the equator. This quantity of rain cools the atmosphere, so as to produce a temperature much more moderate than that which prevails when the sun removes to a greater distance; and, so far as has been ascertained, the tropical regions are perhaps that part of this continent which is best watered. The greater part of the immense deserts—that of the Sahara for instance—lies in general too far north ever to be under the influence of a vertical sun. The torrid zone may therefore be considered as having only two seasons,—the dry and the wet, -which are likewise distinguished, in Western Africa, as the summer and winter. In some districts, indeed, there are two dry and two wet seasons in the year; and these are called the short and the long seasons. In the countries from Cape Blanco up to the Senegal, the mean temperature from November to the end of March is, at 6 A. M., about 73°, and at noon, in the shade, 87°. Farther into the interior of the country-at Bambuk, for instance-the heat is much more intense. On the Gambia, in the same months, the mean temperature, at 6 A.M., is 77°, and at noon, in the shade, 91°. In the months of April, May, and June, on the Senegal, the thermometer, at 6 A. M., indicates 83°, and at noon, in the shade, 95°. From the month of July to the end of October, the mean temperature, at 6 A. M., is 95°, and at noon 107°. In the more southern countries the heat is still greater, and also in the sandy plains, whose fiery aridness and unrelenting sterility can be fully conceived of only by those who have traversed them. In some districts the heat is frequently rendered insupportable by peculiar localities. Thus at Ombos and Syene, in Egypt, the sand, under the influence of a noon-day sun, absolutely scorches the feet of the traveller, and eggs may be dressed by burying them in the sand. At Algiers the mean temperature is 72°; at the cape of Good Hope, in the months of December, January, and February, the thermometer frequently rises to 95° or 98°, and often much higher; but change of temperature is very quickly effected here, and a burning day is frequently followed by a chilly night. During eight months of the year constant fine weather is prevalent throughout a great part of Africa. The sun rises every morning in a clear atmosphere, and spreads a glaring light over the whole country, too brilliant almost for the eye to sustain; no cloud casts a passing shadow over the landscape; and, in the evening, the orb of day sinks magnificently into the ocean. But the excessive heat diminishes the pleasure man might feel in contemplating the glorious sky; and the first clouds which foretell the approach of rain are hailed with delight by the European resident overwhelmed by the oppressive heat.—"At sea, between the tropic of Capricorn and the cape of Good Hope, on both sides of the continent, the transparency of the atmosphere exceeds what is known in any other part of the world and European astronomers, on first visiting these latitudes, contemplate with astonishment the nocturnal splendour of the heavens, in which the naked eye can perceive stars of two degrees less magnitude than it can discern in the northern skies. Here Jupiter and Venus shine with startling refulgence, and cause opaque bodies to cast well-defined shadows; the fixed star Aldebaran appears nearly as ruddy as Mars; Castor and Pollux seem to reflect each other's light; the Corona Australis, dim and obscure in other latitudes, displays the lustre of its gems; and Orion appears preternaturally brilliant and gigantic" [How- Winds.] The limits of the changeable winds of A. are about the 30th degree on each side of the equator: within this region are the passage-winds. These blow more or less NE in the northern hemisphere, and SE in the southern. The monsoons, which are strong and regular in the open Arabian sea, become changeable on approaching the land. In the Arabian sea they generally blow from the E during the months and intervening months of October and May; during the rest of the year they blow from the W. In the Red sea the SE wind prevails in the southern parts, from October to June, when the N wind begins to blow, and lasts during the remainder of the year. In the northern parts of this sea violent N winds prevail for nine months of the year.—The transition from one season to another is generally accompanied by violent hurricanes and thunderstorms. Some districts are more exposed to these visitations than others; as, for instance, the countries between Cape Verga and Cape Monte, which are sometimes visited betwixt the months of June and October by dreadful tornadoes, the effects of which seldom extend to the neighbouring coasts.- In the deserts the wind is often very troublesome to the traveller, by raising the sand, and filling the air with dust, so as to render it impossible to keep one's eyes open, and difficult even to breathe.-Mr. Buckingham, while travelling betwixt the Red sea and the Mediterranean in 1814, encountered one of these sand-tempests, which he has described with great beauty and effect. "The morning," he says, "was delightful on our setting out, and promised us a fine day; but the light airs from the south soon increased to a gale, the sun became obscure, and as every hour brought us into a looser sand, it flew around us in such whirlwinds, with the sudden gusts that blew, that it was impossible to proceed. We halted, therefore, for an hour, and took shelter under the lee of our beasts, who were themselves so terrified as to need fastening by the knees, and uttered in their wailings but a melancholy symphony. I know not whether it was the novelty of the situation that gave it additional horrors, or whether the habit of magnifying evils to which we are unaccustomed, had increased its effect; but certain it is, that fifty gales of wind at sea appeared to me more easy to be encountered than one amongst those sands. It is impossible to imagine desolation more complete; we could see neither sun, earth, nor sky: the plain at ten paces distance was absolutely imperceptible: our beasts, as well as ourselves, were so covered as to render breathing difficult; they hid their faces in the ground, and we could only uncover our own for a moment, to behold this chaos of mid-day darkness, and wait impatiently for its abatement. Alexander's journey to the temple of Jupiter Ammon, and the destruction of the Persian armies of Cambyses in the Libyan desert, rose to my recollection with new impressions, made by the horror of the scene before me; while Addison's admirable lines, which I also remembered with peculiar force on this occasion, seemed to possess as much truth as beauty:- 'Lo! where our wide Numidian wastes extend, Sudden the impetuous hurricanes descend, Which through the air in circling eddies play, Tear up the sands, and sweep whole plains away. The helpless traveller, with wild surprise, Sees the dry desert all around him rise: And, smothered in the dusty whirlwind, dies.' "The few hours we remained in this situation were passed in unbroken silence: every one was occupied with his own reflections, as if the reign of terror forbade communication. Its fury spent itself, like the storms of ocean, in sudden lulls and squalls: but it was not until the third or fourth interval that our fears were sufficiently conquered to address each other; nor shall I soon lose the recollection of the impressive manner in which that was done. 'Allah Kereem!' exclaimed the poor Bedouin, although habit had familiarised him with these resistless blasts. 'Allah Kereem!' repeated the Egyptians, with terrified solemnity: and both my servant and myself, as if by instinct, joined in the general exclamation. The bold imagery of the Eastern poets, describing the deity as avenging in his anger, and terrible in his wrath, riding upon the wings of the wind, and breathing his fury in the storm, must have been inspired by scenes like these."-In Egypt a S wind prevails in summer, which raises immense quantities of sand, and is often so hot as to stop respiration.—Another called samiel by the natives is still hotter and more terrible.—But the most dreadful of all these burning winds is the simoom, which seems to be a concentrated column of the positive electric fluid, moving northwards, from the S or SE, and carrying sure destruction to all who breath the baleful atmosphere which accompanies it. The only chance of escaping destruction when the simoom glides across the desert is, for the traveller to throw himself flat on his face, which he has not always time to do, for it moves with amazing rapidity. Bruce—whose ardent mind was not easily deterred from the attainment of knowledge by the presence of danger—has described this fearful phenomenon. On the attendants calling out that the simoom was coming, he immediately turned for a moment to the quarter whence it came. It resembled a haze, in colour like the purple part of the rainbow, but not so compressed or thick. It was a kind of blush upon the air, and was about 20 yards in breadth, and about 4 from the ground. Its motion was so rapid, that before he could turn and fall upon the ground, he felt its violent heat upon his face. It passed like a gentle rustling wind, but was succeeded by a slight breeze, which for two or three hours was of such intensity of heat, as nearly to suffocate them. Bruce unfortunately inhaled a little of the purple haze, which nearly deprived him of his voice, and caused an asthma of two years' continuance. They saw it twice afterwards as they journeyed across the desert. The second time, it was more southerly— its edges were less defined, resembling a thin smoke-and it had about a yard in the middle tinged with purple and blue. The third time, it had the same purple and blue appearance, but was preceded by the largest sand pillar they had seen .- One of the most striking phenomena on the Gold Coast is the NE wind called harmattan. It comes on indiscriminately at any hour of the day, at any time of the tide, or at any period of the moon; and continues sometimes only a day or two, sometimes five or six days, and has been occasionally known to last fifteen or sixteen days. There are generally three or four returns of it every season; it blows with a moderate force, not so strongly as the sea-breeze, but somewhat more so than the land-wind. A fog or haze is one of the peculiarities which always accompany a harmattan; extreme dryness is another property of it: no dew falls during its continuance, nor is there the least appearance of moisture in the atmosphere, vegetables of every kind are much injured by it, and the grass withers under its influence. The process of evaporation during this wind proceeds with astonishing rapidity. Natural history.] The natural history of A. necessarily partakes of the limitations and imperfections of its geography. The following notices on this subject are chiefly from the pages of M. Balbi's 'Abrégé de Geographie.' Vegetable kingdom.] Much yet remains to be done before we can consider ourselves in possession of a perfect African Flora Our botanical explorations never having extended any distance from its coasts, the vegetable productions of the interior of this vast continent are almost entirely unknown to us; although considerable accuracy of conjecture with respect to their general character may be attained by comparison of the observations of intelligent travellers with the botanical geography of the surrounding districts. The vegetable productions of the coasts of Barbary present a close affinity to those of the southern provinces of the Spanish peninsula. Thus the olive, orange, and citron, of Tripoli and Algiers rival in luxuriance the similar productions of Andalusia and Valencia. The date-tree in Tripoli attains a height of 100 ft., and bears clusters weighing from 20 to 30 pounds. The higher temperature indeed of this region of A. produces conformations unknown in Southern Europe, but not specifically, seldom even generically, different from those of the latter continent. The vegetation of Cyrenaica closely resembles that of Algiers, and connects the botany of the Atlantic with that of Egypt. The Zizyphus lotus grows so abundantly in this country that some of its ancient inhabitants are represented to have made its fruit their principal article of subsistence, and thus came to be distinguished by the Greeks by the name of Lotophagi, or 'Lotus-eaters.' The vegetation of Egypt is highly characterised, and presents many indigenous plants which even under the most imperfect developments readily indicate the country to which they belong. Upper Egypt produces several species of cassia, of which the C. obovata and acutifolia, constitute a considerable article of commerce. Besides the date-tree and the Chamærops humilis, a remarkable species of palm occurs here, which Delisle distinguishes by the name of the Doilm palm, or Crucifera Thebaica. The Nile produces many aquatic plants, which spread their large leaves over its surface, and gracefully rear their flowers above its waters Of these, the extinct Nelumbium speciosum, with the Nymphæa lotus and the carulea, frequently occur in the hieroglyphic emblems of the ancient Egyptians. The fruit described by Theophrastus and other ancient authors, under the name of persea, is produced by a tree supposed by Sacy and Delisle to be the Balanites Agyptiaca, a small thorny tree which covers the sandy districts not only of Egypt but of a great extent of Central A., and likewise grows in great abundance in Senegal. The vegetation of Abessinia appears to differ considerably from that of the tropics; but it bears some affinity to that of the Mozambique coast, and of the cape of Good Hope. Bruce. in his travels in this region, found a species of protea, and Salt a pelargonium belonging to genera peculiar to the Cape and to New Holland. Coffee grows indigenously on the African coast of the Red sea near Bab-el-Mandeb, and in the interior, to the south of Abessinia, in the province to which this plant gives the name of kapha. The botany of Upper Egypt and of the regions further to the S, closely resembles that of the W coast of A. But the most extraordinary varieties in vegetable physiognomy are to be met with at the cape of Good Hope, in the numerous families of the erica, protea, pelargonium, mesembryanthemum, ixia, stapelia, &c. These genera consist of a vast number of species, which, with the exception of one or two belonging to the northern coasts, form an assemblage peculiar to the southern extremity of A. The productions of the equatorial region of this continent bear a strong resemblance to those of its west coasts; so that the vegetable geography of the entire district between 16 deg. N lat., and 6 deg. S lat., from the Senegal to the Congo, presents very great uniformity. Here rise the colossal Adansonia digitata, or the baobab of Nubia, the Bombax pentandrum, the Elais Guineensis, &c. The Sterculia acuminata-whose seeds, called by the natives cola, have, it is said, the property of rendering innoxious the most unwholesome water-grows on the coasts of Guinea and Sierra Leone; and the Anona Senegalensis and Chrysobolanus icaco. also very useful trees, abound on the banks of all its rivers such as the banks of the Gambia and Casamanza. In the hot and humid districts of Senegambia, besides the productions common to the similar climates of Upper Egypt, Arabia, &c., there are plants which have been considered peculiar to the Indian archipelago, to Madagascar, and South America. The vegetation of the dry and sandy tracts of Senegal properly so called, resembles that of Egypt and Arabia. The alimentary vegetables cultivated by the natives of the W coast of A., are maize, cassava, two kinds of pulse, of which one is the Cytisus cajan, L., and the other a kind of haricot (dolichos), and the Arachis hypogea. The best fruit-trees are the banana (Musa sapientum), the papaw (Carica papaya), the lemon, orange, tamarind, and the Elais Guineensis, which produces palm-oil. Palm wine is likewise made from this latter plant as well as from the Raphia vinifera, and from a species of corypha. Some botanists consider the greater number of these plants not indigenous to Africa. Thus Mr. R. Brown assigns American extraction to maize, cassava, ananas, papaw, and tobacco,— while the banana, lemon, orange, tamarind, and sugar-cane, he considers as imports from Asia. The interior of equatorial A., as we have already remarked, has hitherto been inaccessible to botanical research; the few specimens of plants published in the flora of Wara and of Benin can afford but a very inadequate idea of the vegetation of that vast district; but comparing them with the botany of Congo, Senegal, and Upper Egypt, some striking affinities appear which tend to illustrate the universal law that the same climateric influences produce the same vegetable conformations.—In the botanical geography of the principal islands which belong to the African seas, the Canaries present a remarkable transition from the European or rather Mediterranean to the equinoctial flora. European forms present themselves, but in the dimensions of the vegetation of the torrid zone; the herb of the Mediterranean becomes a shrub in the isles of the equatorial Atlantic. St. Helena contains a great many plants belonging both to America and Africa, and a considerable number of European derivation to which the climate evinces remarkable adaptation. Madagascar, and the isles of France and of Bourbon, which occupy an intermediate position between the continent of Africa and the Indian archipelago, possess the vegetable productions indigenous to these regions. Madagascar presents, in its western districts, plants belonging to the coast of Africa; and in the eastern, those of the islands of the Indian seas. This island, as well as the isles of France and of Bourbon, contain many plants unknown in other regions, probably because the researches of botanists have never penetrated the corresponding localities of Africa and India. Of these plants the Nephentes distillatoria is one of the most remarkable. Their nearest affinities are to be traced in the botany of the East Indies. The number of orchids is so great in the three great isles of Southern Africa, that they form the exclusive subject of a work published by Dupetit Thouars. Ferns are likewise extremely numerous. Animal kingdom.-Mammalia.] Wagner, in his admirable survey of the geographical distribution of the mammalia, dividing the globe into certain zoological provinces, takes the northern frontier of the Sahara as a line between two distinct African provinces. This line coincides nearly with the parallel of 30° N lat.; all Africa S of it he makes the zoological province of Africa; but those portions situated to the N of it, and comprising Barbary and the other littoral countries bordering on the Mediterranean, he includes within his European province, on the ground that, although African types still appear there, those of Europe are predominant; in this respect the Mediterranean separates N Africa much less from S Europe than does the Sahara from the main of Africa. Indeed, the entire basin of the Mediterranean, including European, Asiatic, and African shores, is peculiar in itself both to animal as well as to vegetable life. The configuration and character of the surface of Africa explains the peculiar fauna of quadrupeds of this country, which in the main resembles that of the Turanian steppes; the forestinhabiting deer are completely wanting, except in the transition province of Barbary, &c., and they are replaced by the antelopes, which are more numerous in Africa than anywhere else. Further there are the wild striped horses, the zebras, the giraffe, and their constant companion, the ostrich. The extraordinary swiftness of these animals, which enables them to seek for their food at great distances, is peculiarly adapted to the immense plains of the country. The scarcity of forests corresponds further with that of the squirrels, those that are found being mostly groundsquirrels. Mice are numerous, as are also hares, which latter prefer steppe-like country to woodland. The immense quantity of game affords food for plenty of carnivorous animals. Upon the whole, the African mammalian fauna approach much nearer that of Southern Asia than that of S America. From Wagner's tables of the species in various portions of Africa the following summary results are calculated, with the exception of the Rodentia, which are based on Waterhouse's labours: W Africa. The figures in parentheses denote the number of species peculiar to Africa.- In the order Simiae Africa is very rich; their chief region, however, is within the tropics, whence they decrease to the N and S, so that in Barbary and Lower Egypt only one species, (Inuus ecaudatus), and at the southern extremity of A. only two species are found. In Madagascar, the place of the true monkeys is supplied by the makis - Carnivora. Bats are extremely numerous in A., which is also the chief seat of the hedgehog family; the shrews are widely distributed, and the European mole has its representative in the chrysochlore, which is said to be entirely destitute of eyes. Of the larger carnivora, the bear is almost entirely wanting, only occurring in Barbary and Abessinia. The true martens are unknown; but otters occur, and the civets are characteristic types of African carnivora. Of the canis family, the shakal is characteristic, differing from the Asiatic one by a paler skin, approaching the colour of the prevailing deserts. Hyenas are true African tenants. It is the principal home of the lion, who here reigns undisturbed as a king over the animal creation, while in Asia his power is divided with the tiger. He is found in all parts of A., except in the NE portions, in the Sahara, and where European civilization has taken ground. The leopard is the principal representative of the cat tribe.-Marsupialia are entirely wanting.-Of the Rodentia, the burrowing kinds are prevailing. Hares are unknown in W Africa; but porcupines extend from the Cape to Barbary.—The six species of Edentata which are known to occur in A. are also peculiar to it. The aardvark (Orycteropus capensis) is essentially burrowing in its habits, and it is the burrows formed by these animals which are so dangerous to the waggons and horses of the Cape colonists - Pachydermata Of wild horses, the Asinine group is peculiar to Asia, and the Hippotigrine group to Africa. All species of these two groups are found in large herds. The zebras extend over Southern Africa northwards to about 10° N lat. A. has its peculiar species of rhinoceros and elephants; and that remarkable animal, the hippopotamus, is one of its most characteristic types, extending from the river Gariep to the Nile, Lake Chad, Senegal. In the genus sus, the warthogs (Phoscochoerus) belong exclusively to Africa, and the hyrax (wabber, daman, dassic of the colonist) is also its true tenant.—Ruminantia. Antelopes occur in all parts, and in great varieties and numbers. Large herds sometimes literally whiten the country as far as the eye can reach over the far-stretching plains. In dry seasons they sometimes pay visits to the farmers in the colony, and become as destructive as locusts. The most valuable of the African animals is the camel or dromedary, distinguished from the Asiatic species or Bactrian camel by having only one bunch. It ranges over the Sahara, and all Northern Africa as far as the Lake Chad, but is everywhere domesticated. Ritter, in his map showing the distribution of the camel, indicates its original native country to be that part of the Sahara lying in the longitude of Lake Chad, and inhabited by the Tibbus. Lastly, the giraffe, one of the most celebrated and characteristic of the African quadrupeds, ranges from the Cape colony as far as the Sahara and Nubia. The ornithology of A. presents a close analogy, in all its geographical varieties, to that of the corresponding districts of Europe and Asia. Thus the ornithology of the region of the Nile, and the coasts of the Mediterranean, is identified with that of Arabia, Persia, and Spain. The deserts of Central Africa are inhabited by species adapted to its solitudes; while its southern extremity possesses races presenting features altogether new and characteristic. The isles of the Atlantic, St Helena, Ascension, and the Cape Verde islands, contain no species distinct from those of the continental coasts.—The ostrich is found in the equatorial, and in all the desert regions of A. The ostrich of South A. is a prudent and wary animal, and displays little of that stupidity ascribed to it by some naturalists. On the borders of the Cape colony, when pursued for the sake of its valuable plumage, this bird displays no want of sagacity in providing for its own safety or the security of its offspring. The beautiful white feathers so highly prized by the ladies of Europe, are found on the tail only of the male bird. The food of the ostrich consists of the tops of the various shrubby plants which even the most arid parts of South A. produce. It is so easily satisfied in regard to water that it is constantly found in the most parched and desolate tracts, which even the antelopes and beasts of prey have deserted. Its cry at a distance is said so much to resemble that of a lion, that even the Hottentots are sometimes deceived by it.—The large messenger or secretary-bird, which preys upon reptiles, is one of the most remarkable African birds. It is common near the Cape. Vultures, griffons, chincous, oricous, and the less formidable perenopteres, are abundant; and eagles are found in every region of Africa. The pygargues, or the species which feed on fish, frequent the banks of the rivers, lakes, and sea-coasts. In addition to these diurnal birds of prey, are kites, sparrow-hawks, vultures, buzzards, and falcons. Bats abound, but Africa has few nocturnal birds. In the warm districts of the Cape are found the beautiful couroucous; and the not less magnificent musophages and touracos are peculiar to this copper-coloured cuckoo, the indicator, celebrated by Levaillant, the angular spurred coucal, and the couas or tailsous, are peculiar sinia and of Madagascar represent the toucans of America. Parparroquet is peculiar to Senegal, and the jaco or grey parrot to the country between the Congo and Guinea. Various species of wood-peckers, king-fishers, and swallows, are found in all parts of Africa. The soui-mangas represent the humming-birds of the New World. The crow, particularly the large-beaked crow, here presents some remarkable species. Of blackbirds, one species is remarkable for its bronze colour or brownish plumage. But of all the African families of birds, the sparrows are the most numerous; and of their hundreds of species, the weaver-bird, which connects this tribe with the turipiales of America, is one of the most interesting. Bull-finches and the common sparrow abound in all the districts which produce the seeds which form their appropriate sustenance. The Cape possesses a pretty species of the titmouse. Pigeons are found in all parts of A.; the green pigeon, or waalia of Bruce, is common in Abessinia. Besides the larger species of the poultry of India, A. possesses the pintades or Numidian fowl; and its sterile sandy districts, corresponding to the steppes of the plains of Asia, are the adopted retreats of the partridge and heathcock. The bustards, the long-legged crane, the elegant marabouts, and the anthropoides or Balearic crane, are confined to the deserts. The banks of the rivers abound with the aquatic birds common to tropical countries. Of the web-footed tribes, besides the anhinga, which is peculiar to Africa, cormorants, pelicans, petrels, albatrosses, swans, and ducks, are numerous. Reptiles are not so numerous in Africa as in the same latitudes of the other continents.-Crocodiles are very common in the Nile, Niger, and Senegal. The crocodile, like the seahorse, is an inhabitant both of the land and of the water. Unlike the hippopotamus, however, which seldom makes an attack till provoked, the crocodile adds cunning to strength, and incessantly exerts both to satisfy a voracious appetite. He grows to a great size: being, it is said, sometimes not less than 25 ft. in length, and upwards of 5 ft. in girth. The head is long; the mouth is large in proportion to the body, and armed with a row of formidable teeth; the whole of the body is covered with scales, which are soft under the belly and hard on the back; resemble, in their conformation, the arms of a man. The toes, which have some resemblance to fingers, are connected by a strong membrane, and armed with large claws. The colour of the back is a dark brown; of the belly a whitish citron; the tended upon land, no small resemblance to a fallen tree. In their mother, are by her conveyed thither. But of the great number of eggs very few ever arrive at maturity; for both the eggs and the young of crocodiles are fortunately a grateful prey to many creatures.-Venomous serpents, especially the horned cerastes, abound at the Cape. A few species of lizards, geckos, and batraciens, belong to this continent; and immense tortoises frequent the lagunes and the volcanic islands of the Atlantic ocean.—The fish of Africa, with the exception of those of Egypt, are still very imperfectly known. The fresh waters of Egypt produce the gigantic bichir, the coffres, and numerous species of the pimelodes. The greater number of the fish of the Red sea resemble the saxatiles of the warm seas of Asia. On the west coast are found the fish belonging to equatorial latitudes, while the shores of the Mediterranean produce those of France and Spain. The seas of the southern extremity possess the species common to the latitudes of the Antarctic south of the three great capes. The fish of the east coast between the tropics are the same as those of the Indian seas. The different regions of the continent and of its coasts present equal variety of mollusca. The marine shells on the east coast within the equator approximate to those of the Indian ocean. The Nile affords, according to M. Cailliaud, an excellent species of oyster. The mollusca of the banks of the Mediterranean correspond with those of Italy and Sardinia. The purple murex of the Romans is here extremely abundant.—The insects of Africa are so extremely numerous, and present such an immense variety of species, corresponding with its different climateric influences, that it is impossible to present an enumeration of them in a work of this description.—Of the class of zoophytes, the brilliant polypi of every variety, and madrepores, abound on the coasts of Africa. The shores of the Mediterranean produce the finest coral. The Red sea is bristled with extensive reefs of the same mollusca Minerals.] We have of course only a very imperfect acquainttance with the geology and mineralogy of Africa. It appears that primitive rocks, transition rocks, floetz, alluvium, and volcanic mountains have been found upon this continent. Salt is obtained in great quantity on both sides of the Atlas.
