It is found either covering the soil, or disposed in thick strata. Mineral salt has also been discovered in South A. Barrow says that coals have been found in the Tigre mountains, N of the cape of Good Hope. Rock-crystal occurs in Tunis; cornelian and chalcedony are found in Egypt; jasper, agate, and blue and yellow amianthus at the Orange river. Graphite is said to have been found at the Cape, and likewise in Tunis. Iron exists in abundance in all the Mandara hills, and also in Marocco. There are some silver-mines in Tunis, and others are said to exist behind Mozambique and Congo. Copper is found in the Western Atlas, and quicksilver in Tunis. But the article of exchange which A. produces most abundantly, particularly between the 15th and 22d parallels of southern latitude, is gold. Mr. Jackson states-with considerable exaggeration surely—that at Timbuctu this precious metal is often exchanged for its weight in salt, tobacco, or other valuable commodities. All the gold that occurs upon the W coast of A. is found in the state of dust, and is obtained by washing the soil; but immense quantities have been collected in this way for nearly four centuries. In Tripoli the precious ore is sifted from the sands on the coast. Population.] The population of the African continent is of course more difficult to ascertain, even by approximation, than its superficial extent. We have no census of a single known kingdom or town; and the existence of others is still matter of conjecture. Pinkerton thinks there are not above 30,000,000 of human beings in Africa; Malte Brun supposes they cannot exceed 70,000,000; Gräberg estimates them at 99,000,000; Golberry says they may amount to 160,000,000; and Ukert, allowing one-third of surface for uninhabited territory, approximates the population of the remaining two-thirds to above 100,000,000 souls. We subjoin a table of Ukert and Gräberg's calculations:- According According to Ukert. to Graberg. The history of by far the greater part of this mysterious country is yet enveloped in darkness, and the origin of its inhabitants utterly unknown. Ritter maintains that A. has only two principal races of aborigines,—an opinion which the Father of history had already pronounced, and which recent researches, we must allow, rather tend to confirm than invalidate. These two original tribes are the inhabitants of the mountainous districts, called by the ancients Ethiopians, and by the moderns Negroes,—and the inhabitants of the plains, known to the former under the name of Libyans, and to the latter under that of Barbars or Berbers, and belonging to the great Caucasian stem according to Blumenbach. The Negro tribes.] The Negroes, or inhabitants of the mountains—who have also extended themselves in many districts to the sea-coast-have preserved themselves free from all admixture of foreign blood. They are an isolated portion of the human race, although they have divided themselves into a number of distinct tribes, amongst whom an amazing variety of tongues and dialects are spoken. They seem to have gradually descended from the mountain-seats of their ancestors, passing from terrace to terrace of this curiously configured country, and driving the inhabitants of the lower districts before them, or in some rare instances—as in the case of the Mandingoes-intermarrying and amalgamating with them. There is no trace of their ever having existed in a higher state of civilization than they do at this moment; and we have but a few philological indications to guide us in tracing the progress and affiliation of a few of their tribes. The Negro tribes are of every shade from dark brown to jet black. The Caffres and Hottentots are brown. The children of Europeans and Negroes are called Mulattoes, and their colour varies from a light to a dark brown. The children of Europeans and mulattoes are called Tercerons; those of Tercerons and Europeans, Quadroons. The children of Negroes and Americans are called Zambos or Sambos. The Ethiopian race is distinguished by a dark brown or jet black complexion, woolly hair, a small head, a prominent forehead and projecting eyes, a thick flat nose, thick lips, and high cheek-bones. They are a mild, and peaceable race, hospitable and inclined to ease and indulgence, but timid and indolent withal. Pinkerton does them gross injustice when he describes them as being "akin to ferocious animals." Malte Brun's assertion that the Negro, notwithstanding his degraded condition in many places, "is not unacquainted with any of those feelings which dignify and exalt human nature," is supported by ample testimony. The Berber tribes.] The inhabitants of the plains, -amongst whom Ritter regards the Berbers as the main stem,-have, according to that sometimes fan- A Brown Se VII П IX VIII . 2 æ . A. I ciful but profound geographer, taken an impress of character from the mutable and ever-alternating features of those regions in which they reside. For thousands of years there has been a process of evershifting change effecting upon the surface of a vast portion of the African plains; the soil seems to have been driven from hill to hill in a series of successive undulations from E to W, bearing along with it the tribes which roam over its surface, as well as its streams, and oases. Men do not war with the eternal impulses of Nature; and since the time of the Nasamones mentioned by Herodotus, until the present day, the various tribes of these districts have been in a state of constant movement. This wandering character, Ritter farther contends, manifests itself in the lean figure, the power of abstinence, and the want of industry, displayed by these tribes; in the ease with which they adopt foreign customs; their nomadic life; the flexibility of their character; and finally, in their great adaptation for commercial employments. The Berbers are in general well-made, tall, and thin. Their features are quite different from those of the Negroes; their hair is long, and their colour varies from yellow to black. They are mostly all Mahommedans in religion. Classification of African nations according to language.] Seetzen, who had much intercourse with the natives, supposed that the number of languages spoken in A. might amount to 150, which is probably not an over-estimate. Jackson says 33 languages are spoken between the Western ocean and the Red sea. The Arabian language seems to be the most extensively spread. Marsden and some others have traced the language of the Berbers to the oasis of Siwah, and also to the foot of Mount Atlas,—that is from the extreme E to the extreme W of Northern A.; and he conjectures it may have been the general language of Northern A. before the period of the Mahommedan conquests; and that, so marked is its affinity to certain forms of the Oriental languages, it may not be unreasonable to consider it as connected with the ancient Punic,—an opinion in which M. Langles is disposed to concur.—The following arrangement of the nations and tribes of A., according to their languages, is proposed by Balbi; but is far from presenting a complete and satisfactory ethnographical table, even with the numerous modifications we have introduced into it. REGION OF THE NILE.—In this region we find the following families: 1. The Egyptian family, or Copts, the Christian descendants of the ancient Egyptians. They are now reduced to a small number, not exceeding 150,000. Their language, in consequence of the numerous conversions to Islamism, has, since the middle of the 17th century, become nearly obsolete, and is now understood by few persons. 2. The Nubian family, or the Nubas and the Kenus. These tribes speak different dialects of a language which prevails throughout Lower Nubia to the exclusion of the Arabic. They are black, or nearly so; but do not present the woolly hair nor features of the Negro. A race frequently depicted on ancient Egyptian monuments are supposed to be identical with the people of these tribes. 3. The Troglodytic family, including the Bisharin, the Hadendoa, the Hammadeh, the Amarer, the Adarebs, and the Ababdehs, who are sometimes erroneously identified with the Beduin Arabs. All these families occupy the district of Nubia, situated on the east of the Nile. 4. The Shiho-Dankali family. The Shiho, properly so called, inhabit the region of the pass of Assuali; and the Hazorta that of the pass of Taranta in Abyssinia. The Danakil, or Dankali, are a nomadic race, occupying the coast of the Red sea, from Bab-el-Mandeb to Arkiko. The Adaiel inhabit the country to the S of the Danakil territory, between Bab-el-Mandeb and the environs of Zeila. 5. The Shilluks, known also by the names of Nuba and Fongi, who inhabit the skirts of the Bahr-el-Abiad, and the kingdom of Sennaar, of which, until the recent Egyptian conquest, they were the dominant people. 6. The Tcheret-Agows, who occupy the central district of Abessinia; a brave race and good horsemen. 7. The Furians, who form the mass of the population of Darfur. They have generally short woolly hair, and black complexion; but differ from the Negroes of Guinea. ı. REGION OF THE ATLAS.—The aborigines of this region may all be included under the single head of the Atlantic family, which includes the Amazighs, or Berebers, and the Kabyles, &c., who occupy the upper valleys of the Atlas and a part of the level tracts of Marocco, Algiers, and Tunis. They are divided into several tribes, some of whom retain their independence. The Tuaricks, a numerous and warlike people, described by Lyon as the finest race of men he ever saw, are scattered over the central district of the Sahara; and the Tibbus, a slender and active race, have overrun nearly the eastern district. Oudney describes the Tuarick language as harsh and guttural. The Tibboo is full of liquids. The Siwahan spoken by the inhabitants of Siwah, and the independent tribes of the Shilluks who occupy the southern region of Marocco, appears to be a dialect of the Bereber, or Berber. NEGROLAND, OF THE REGION OF NIGRITIA, presents the following families: 1. The Yolofs, or Iolofs, reputed the handsomest and likewise the blackest of the Negro tribes. They possess the kingdoms of Bourb-i-Yolof, Cayor, and Baol; and form the majority of the population of Bondu, Lower Yani, and Salum. Golberry describes them as a robust well-made race, with regular features, and a fine brilliant pure black complexion. Their language is soft and agreeable. 2. The Mandingo family. The Mandingoes are a powerful, civilized, and industrious people, with nearly the exclusive command of the trade of the country in gold, ivory, and slaves. They possess, besides the extensive territory between the Gambia and Geba, and the coast-district watered by the Kissi, the kingdoms of Bambuk, Kasson, Kaarta, Barra, Kollar, Badibu, Upper Yani, Woolli, Dentilia, and Kabu, in Senegambia. They also constitute the majority of the population, and before its disruption were the dominant people of the empire of Bambara in the western district of Central Nigritia. The Susu or Suzes, a civilized tribe, belonging to the Mandingo family, occupy the western coast between the Nunez and Kissi, besides other districts of Senegambia. Major Laing describes the Mandingoes as a shrewd people, and superior to any of Western Africa from the boundaries of Marocco southward, 3. The Foulahs, or Fellatahs, called also Foulans, Poules, &c., a very numerous and powerful tribe, are scattered over almost the entire region of Eastern Nigritia, in which they possess Fouta-Toro, the kingdom of Bondu, Fouta- Jalo, Fouladu, and Bruko; in Central Nigritia or Sudan they occupy Wassela, Sangara, &c., as well as the extensive empire which bears their name. Denham describes the Fellatahs, bordering on Mandara, as a handsome race of a deep copper colour. Lyon says their complexion is little darker than that of the English gypsies. 4. The Jalontees form a large proportion of the population of Fouta-Jalo, Kouronia, Baleya, Firia, Sangara, Sulimana, and Boure. 5. The Kissours inhabit the kingdom of Timbuktu in Sudan. 6. The Kalannas inhabit the kingdom of the same name in Sudan. 7. The Haoussa family compose the majority of the population of the provinces of Kashna, Gouber or Ghoober, Kano, Dourg, &c., belonging to the extensive territory of Haoussa, which forms the nucleus of the empire of the Fellahs or Fellatah. 8. The Yarribas are the dominant people in the kingdom of the same name. 9. The Mandaras inhabit the kingdom of Mandara in Sudan. 10. The Baghermehs and Mobbas are the dominant tribes in the two kingdoms of the same name in Sudan. 11. The Bornouese family form the mass of the population of Bornou properly so called, and of some other districts which have been detached from that empire by the Fellatah conquest. 12. The Timmanees occupy the territory between the Scarcies river and Cape Shilling, in which the colony of Sierra Leone is situated. 13. The Bullam inhabit the district stretching along the coast from the Timmanees to the frontiers of the kingdom of Cape Monte and towards the interior. They are likewise numerous in the adjacent islands. 14. The Ashantees are the dominant tribe of the empire of Ashantee, and also form a considerable proportion of the population of all its subsidiary kingdoms. Their language is widely diffused, and the Fantee, Warsaw, Akim, Assim, and Aquapim are dialects of it. 15. The Dagomba family embraces several tribes to the north of the Ashantees. 16. The Akkras, or Inkrans, inhabit the kingdom of the same name, and are tributaries to the Ashantees. 17. The Kerrapus, a numerous tribe, are scattered over several small states tributary to the Ashantees. 18. The Ardra family includes the Dahomeys, who are the dominant tribe in the kingdom of Dahomey; the Judahs and Ardras who inhabit territories of the same name, of which the former is tributary to the kingdom of Dahomey, the latter to that of Yarriba; and the Benins who are the dominant people in the kingdom of the same name, and who occupy the greater extent of its vast territory. 18. The Kayli family embraces the Kaylis, and the Gungomes, who inhabit territories of the same name near the coast of Gabon. 19. The Congo family includes the inhabitants of Congo, properly so called, and of Sonho, Cacongo, Loango, Mayumba, Oando, &c., who speak different dialects of the Congo tongue; and the people of Ho, Concabella, and Sala, whose language is a compound of the Abunda and Congo. Sala -the Anzico and Micoco of our maps-is represented by Douville as one of the most considerable states in this region of Africa. The Molouas, whose immense kingdom is reckoned the most powerful of all the African states south of the equator, and the inhabitants of Moucangama, Muchingi, Humé, Cassanga, Cutato, Ginga, Holo-ho, Bailundo, Bihê, and of the Portuguese territory of Angola, all speak dialects of the Abunda language. Douville considers the Molouas the head of this great branch of the Congo family; and ranks them, with the Bihé, highest in the scale of Negro tribes for intelligence and industry. 20. The Benguela family includes the inhabitants of the Portuguese territory of Benguela, those of Quisama, Libolo, Quigné, Nano, Humbe, Monganguela, &c., all of whom, according to Douville, speak the dialects or tongues belonging to this family. THE REGION OF SOUTH AFRICA presents the following families: 1. The Kaffir or Caffre family, which extending from Natal on the coast into the interior, and probably as far as the tropic, includes the Koussas, Tambukies, and Mambukies, in Maritime Caffraria; and the Betchuanas subdivided into Briquas, Tammahas, Barrolongs, Maquainas, Morolongs, and Gokas, in Interior Caffraria. Their colour is a blackish grey, and they are generally well-made. 2. The Hottentot family includes the Korannas, Gonaquas, Namaquas, Damaras, and other tribes of the Hottentot family properly so called, who inhabit the territory of the Cape of Good Hope, and the independent Hottentot territory along the basin of the Orange river. Their colour is dark brown, or yellowish. Their hair is black, short, and woolly. The Saabs, commonly called Bosjesmans or Bushmen, the most uncivilized and degraded of the tribes of Southern Africa, roam on the northern frontiers of the colony of the Cape. EASTERN AFRICA contains the following families, several of whom, however, as the Gallas, by the distribution of the vast territory which they occupy, belong likewise to the region of the Nile; or to the region of Sudan, as the Nineanai and the Gingiros. 1. The Monomatapa family, comprehending the Mongas, who inhabit the environs of Sena; the Bororo, who inhabit the country between Sena and Tete; the Movizas; the Maravi, now the most powerful of the tribes of the former empire of Monomatapa; the Macouas, a very powerful Negro race who inhabit the coast and inland country west of Mozambique, extending northerly to the environs of Melinda, and southerly to the mouth of the Zambeze; the Monjous, one of the ugliest of the Negro tribes, in the interior near the Southern Macouas; and the Sowaiel, or Sowauli, a very powerful Negro family, who occupy the coast from Magadoxo to opposite Mombaza. 2. The Galla family, a numerous, powerful, and warlike race, are the dominant tribe throughout the greater extent of the former empire of Abessinia. They likewise appear to occupy all the country extending southerly from Abyssinia to the eastern frontiers of the states situated along the coast between Melinda and Magadoxo. Belonging to this family are the Mouzimbes, or Zimbes, a nomadic race, who roam over the vast district supposed to be watered by the Zebee, and who acquired notoriety towards the end of the 16th century for the incursive warfare which they carried as far as Melinda and Quiloa. 3. The Somaulis, who occupy the country which stretches triangularly along the coast of Aden, from Cape Guardafui towards the interior. 4. The Gingiros, who inhabit the kingdom of the same name, supposed to be situated to the south of the mountains of Abessinia and on the banks of the Zebee. 6. The Nineanai, who occupy the country of Bomba. Identity of name, and the local position assigned to it by Douville, would appear to identify Bomba with the country of the Maniemougi, with respect to which geographers have hitherto offered nothing but conjecture. Besides these aboriginal races, various families are to be found in Africa which have sprung into existence at different epochs. Thus the Abessinian tribes, who speak the Gheez or Tigre language, in the kingdom of Tigre, and the Amhara in the kingdoms of Amhara, Ankober, and Angote, belong incontestably to the Semitic branch; and appear to have settled in these localities from time immemorial. Thus too the Arabs, at a very remote period, and more recently by the conquest of the immediate successors of Mahomet, have been gradually filling the valley of the Nile in Egypt, the greater portion of Nubia, and the countries of Shendy, Damer, Scheygya, &c., along the banks of the Nile: and are to be found in all the towns of the Barbary states, in the eastern districts of Nigritia, and throughout the greater extent of country surrounding the Sahara. They have likewise established themselves in some of the states of Central Nigritia, such as Darfur, Mobba, Baghermi, and the empires of Bornu and of the Fellatahs. They are likewise to be met with all along the eastern coast of this great continent, and on the adjacent isles; and on the west coast of the great island of Madagascar. The Osmanli, or Ottoman Turks, have recently become the dominant nation in the lower part of the region of the Nile. The Madecasses, or Malgaches, who compose the mass of the population of the island of Madagascar, belong incontest. ably to the great Malay nation who are scattered over the entire extent of Oceania; but the period of their arrival in that island is anterior to historical epochs. The numerous families into which the Malay race is divided will be specified elsewhere. Europe, since the era of the Greeks and Romans, has made important contributions to the population of Africa. The Portuguese, Spaniards, French, English, Dutch, Danes, and Anglo-Americans, possess settlements in Africa. Religions.] Fêtichism of the lowest and most debasing description is the prevailing religion of Africa, being professed by nearly all the tribes of the Negro and Atlantic families, and by the aborigines of Madagascar. The existence of lucky and unlucky days, and of a good and evil principle, is generally admitted by them, and they hold the juggleries of a degraded priesthood in high estimation. Some tribes have a national and supreme fetiche. The Whydahs or Widahs, for example, adore the serpent, and maintain an order of priests and priestesses in a temple dedicated to that reptile. The fierce Bissagos pay divine homage to the cock; and the people of Benin, who worship their own shadow, make a divinity of the lizard. The rivers Tando, Cobi, and the Rio- Volta or Adieri, are held in sacred veneration by the inhabitants of their respective localities; while by other tribes various species of animals are worshipped as deities. The cataracts of the Bousempra are the supreme fetiche of the Negroes of the Gold coast; and the Agows have from time immemorial paid divine honours to the genius of the Nile. The inhabitants of Wassena worship the moon; while the fetiche of several tribes in the region of the Nile, in the interior near Cape Mesurado, and in Guinea, is the sun. Trees, stones, the moon, and some of the stars, are objects of worship to the fierce and powerful Gallas. Some Negro tribes confer the human physiognomy upon their idols, and these have been observed to bear a striking resemblance to the European or Egyptian figure, or rather to that of the ancient Etruscan statues. The office of highpriest is held in great veneration by the Betchuanas, and is ranked by them next to that of the king. Fêtichism prevails in the kingdom of Dagomba, although adherence to the Koran is professed by the court; and its celebrated oracle has rendered it an emporium of commerce. The religion of Magadoxo is a compound of Mahommedanism and Paganism; that of Noki, an incongruous medley of Christianity and fetichism. Amongst the anthropophagi of Central and Southern Nigritia, the immolation of human victims, in their idolatrous rites, is of frequent occurrence. - Mahommedanism prevails in all the principal states of the Moghreb, and throughout the greater extent of the region of the Nile; in some districts also of the east coast and of Sudan. Not far from Lake Djibou or Dibbie, is a small temple to which Mahommedan pilgrims are wont to resort. The Foulahs of Fouta-Toro, and of Fouta-Jalon, the Susus, and almost all the Mandingoes, are bigoted Mahommedans. The latter have extended Islamism to Sierra Leone and Dahomey .- Christianity, in connexion with the Greek or Oriental church, is professed by all the Coptic tribes, or rather by the Monophysites who form the majority of the population of Abessinia, and by the majority of the Copts resident in Egypt. To the Roman Catholic church belong the inhabitants of the Spanish and a large proportion of those of the Portuguese and French colonies, and some of the Copts of Egypt. The reformed religion of the Lutheran and Calvinistic churches prevails in the English, Danish, Dutch, and Anglo-American colonies.—Judaism is adhered to by the numerous Jews who are scattered over the Barbary states, Egypt, and Abessinia.—Magism is professed by a few Guebres at Mozambique.—Paganism, modified by notions derived from the Arabs, of a good and evil principle, appears to constitute the religious creed of the inhabitants of Madagascar. Commerce and Trade.] None of the other great continents are so favourably situated for commerce as Africa. Placed almost in the centre of the world, it enjoys an easy communication with Europe, Asia, and America. For almost 1,000 m. from E to W, its northern shores lie opposite to the southern shores of Europe; while the intervening distance is nowhere greater than 100 leagues, and in some places does not exceed 20. The Arabian gulf affords communication with Asia, for a considerable extent from N to S, at a distance of from 5 to 50 leagues; and though separated by a wider interval from the southern coast of Asia, the interposition of islands from Madagascar to Malabar, as well as the alternation of the trade-winds, render that interval particularly facile for navigation. Upwards of 2,000 m. of its western coast lie opposite to America and the Western islands, at the distance of from 500 to 700 leagues. Unfortunately, however, those advantages are more than counterbalanced by one great deficiency peculiar to this continent. Penetrated by no inland seas, like those of Europe and Asia, -overspread by no extensive lakes, as North America, -nor containing, like all the other great continents, navigable rivers flowing from the centre to the extremities,-its regions are separated from each other by the least surmountable of all boundaries, arid deserts of formidable extent, which cannot be traversed without imminent danger and severe suffering. Hence the progress of civilization in A. has been beyond comparison slower than in any other quarter of the globe. At a very remote period, indeed, some of its nations were eminent for their opulence, and their acquaintance with the liberal arts. Egypt was long the luminary of the world, whence the rays of knowledge diverged in various directions, enlightening with peculiar splendour the states of Greece. Ancient writers have been fond of celebrating the superior refinement of some of the Ethiopian nations; and we know that Carthage stood long unrivalled in power, in opulence, and in the arts of elegance and ingenuity to which extensive commerce gives birth. The conquests of the Romans extended civilization throughout the north of A.; and, during the period of Roman dominion, many native Africans rose to distinguished eminence in the various departments of literature. The unexpected appearance of wealth and refinement which has been observed in some nations of the interior, and the superior skill which they are reported to possess in a few of the arts of manufacture, have led an ingenious writer to suppose that in some of those isolated empires, the arts, and perhaps the language of ancient Egypt may still imperfectly survive; or that the Carthaginians—who do not appear to have perished with their cities-may have retired to the southern parts of Africa, and, though lost to the world in the vast oblivion of the Desert, may have carried with them to the yet unexplored regions which they there occupy, some portion of those arts and sciences, and of that commercial knowledge, for which the inhabitants of Carthage were once so eminently famed. We would gladly cherish such a pleasing supposition; but to us, we own, it appears improbable that the Carthaginians, to whom the coasts of Africa-with which they were already acquainted—offered a more inviting, and almost as safe an asylum, should ever have attempted to penetrate the Desert; besides, our ideas of the magnificence and civilization of many of those far-famed states have been considerably lowered by the communications of recent travellers. To the Africans, in short, the arts which improve and embellish life are almost wholly unknown. The nations in the interior have scarcely advanced beyond a state of nature; and the striking difference which travellers remark in their manners and dispositions, seems wholly to result, either from the difference of their religious sentiments, or from original constitution. While enumerating the causes which have retarded the progress of commerce, and the arts and habits of civilized life in A., we must not omit to notice one which, more perhaps than all others, has blighted the hopes of humanity for this unfortunate continent. It will remain an indelible reproach on the name of Europeans, that, for more than three centuries, their intercourse with A., instead of imparting to the natives the blessings of religion, of industry, and civilization, has tended only to destroy their happiness and debase their character. The accursed slave-trade has naturally excited their detestation against Christians; while the arts of the slave-merchant have excited the cupidity of the various native tribes, and heightened their ferocity, by increasing the frequency of their wars. But we must not enter into a detail of the enormities to which this abominable traffic has given rise. Rather let us hope, that ere the current century closes, A. may emerge, under the bright and unquenchable beams of Christian knowledge, from the darkness which has so long enshrouded it; and become once more the cradle of great and prosperous nations. The commerce of the Africans is chiefly confined to the interior of their country; they seldom trade with foreign countries, and their commerce by sea is mere cabotage or coast-carriage; indeed, in none of the African states is the art of ship-building practised. The merchants who frequent distant markets form themselves into companies called caravans. The number of camels accompanying a caravan is generally from between the extremes of 500 and 2,000; they travel at the rate of 3 miles an hour, and 6 or 7 hours in each day. The following are some of the principal routes pursued by these caravans: 1st, From Murzuk, the capital of Fezzan, to Cairo, a route of about 40 days. The halting-places are Siwah, Aujelah and Temissa. 2d, From Murzuk to Bornu, a journey of 50 days. The track is through the deserts of Bilma and Tibesti; and the principal halts are at Temissa, Dombu, and Kanem. 3d, From Murzuk to Kashna. This occupies 60 days, through Hiatts, Ganatt, and Agades. 4th, From Fez to Timbuktu, a journey of 54 days; but as 65 besides are spent in rest, the whole number of days required is 119. The stations are Akka or Tatta, Tegazza, and Arawan or Aroan. Another route along the sea-coast, leads to the same point by Wad Nun, Cape Bojador, and Gualata. 5th and 6th, The caravans from Senaar and Darfur to Egypt. These do not travel so regularly as the others; an interval of two or three years often elapses betwixt them. Of the commerce of South Africa we have few and very imperfect accounts. The common media of exchange are bullion in the N, and gold-dust, ingots, and cowries in the S. With many Negro tribes, European merchandise and glass-beads form the only medium of exchange; but salt is the chief basis of trade from Northern to Central Africa. The first, and by far the most considerable object of African export, in modern times, is the human species; and, to their eternal disgrace, Europeans have ever been the principal abettors in this most atrocious traffic. In some years as many as upwards of 120,000 slaves have been transported from the shores of A. across the Atlantic; while the slave-marts of the Barbary states, Egypt, and Arabia, are kept plentifully supplied from Nubia, Abyssinia, and the unexplored districts to the south of these countries. The victims of the foreign slave-trade are drawn chiefly from the tribes inhabiting the heights of Central Africa. Gold-dust is the second article of exchange which Africa produces; and next to it ivory. Both are procured solely in the interior, and thence brought down to the coast. Palm-oil is now exported to a considerable amount. Gum Senegal is another important object of export. It exudes from a species of acacia, of which the southern parts of the Sahara contain vast forests. Under the vigorous administration of the present pasha of Egypt, cotton is grown and exported to a considerable extent, besides rice, flax, linseed, and opium. Hides, skins, ornamental woods, and dye-woods, complete the list of principal African exports. For internal consumption, cottoncloths, mats, and leather, are made by some tribes for their own use; and the smith furnishes rude implements to the agriculturist, arms to the warrior, and ornaments to the chiefs and women. Knowledge of the Ancients regarding Africa.] The geographical knowledge which the ancients possessed of the African coast, is supposed to have extended, on the western shore, to Cape Blanco or Cape de Verde; on the eastern, to the island of Pembo. The valley of the Nile was known in early history under the name it now bears; but the neighbouring countries were veiled in darkness to the ancients, and comprehended under the general name of Nigritia or Negroland. The Greeks, according to Herodotus, and after them the Romans, having acquired a better knowledge of the coasts of the Mediterranean, may perhaps have sailed up the Joliba; but they appear never to have penetrated farther than to the limits of Numidia, and to have been totally unacquainted with Southern Africa. Knowledge of the Arabians.] This continent, as containing some of the principal seats of Mahommedan power, early attracted the attention of Arabian geographers; some of whom visited the interior, and still remain almost our only authorities with regard to certain districts. The bigotry of Islamism, however, was opposed to Arabian research in many quarters of this continent. "Of Christians and Ethiopians," says Ibn Haukal, in his 'Oriental Geography,' "I have spoken little, for my innate love of justice, religion, and good government, made it impossible for me to find in these people any thing deserving of praise, or even of mention." The same sapient reasons seem to have prevailed with most of Ibn Haukal's colleagues, in the task of African geography; and thus the limits of Moslem dominion were with most of them the limits likewise of any thing like correct geographical knowledge of this country. At some distant period the Arabians penetrated across the great Desert to the eastern shores of the Niger, where they established several extensive kingdoms, amongst which Gana—the modern Kano-held a decided pre-eminence. Westwards from Gana, at the distance of about 400 m., Tocrur—the modern Sokkatu— and Sala, are described as flourishing Mahommedan cities situated on the Niger. The countries beyond were regarded as a wide expanse of trackless desert. To the east of Gana lay Wangara, or 'the Country of gold.' The Arabians knew little of Nubia, and seem to have confounded the river of Bornu with the Nile. They had flourishing settlements on the eastern coast; but seem to have had no acquaintance with the cape of Good Hope. Edrisi, in his map, extends Africa till it becomes conterminous with India and China. Progress of European discovery.] From the 10th to the 14th century constituted the golden age of Arabian science; from the 15th century downwards, the honours of geographical discovery have belonged to Europe. Cape Nun, in the same parallel with the Canary islands, was discovered by Henry, the Navigator, in 1412. The Canary islands had been discovered in 1402, and from that period the spirit of maritime discovery was almost wholly engrossed by the Portuguese, whose discoveries completed the outlines of this continent, long before much was known regarding the interior. The Portuguese themselves had penetrated into the country only at the two opposite points of Abessinia and Congo; but, misled by the notices of Edrisi, they placed the source of the Nile in Abessinia, and the mountains of the Moon midway between the tropic of Capricorn and the cape of Good Hope; while the lake Zambia or Dembea was placed about 2,000 miles south of its real position, and described as "the great mother and chief lady of all the African waters."-About the commencement of the 18th century, the English and the French began to form settlements on the Gambia and Senegal; and about 1720 Francis Moore travelled into the interior. He adopted the geographical notions of Leo and Edrisi, and regarded the Gambia and Senegal as the two mouths by which the Niger, after traversing nearly the whole breadth of Africa, discharged itself into the ocean.—In 1754 D'Anville read several memoirs on African geography before the French academy of sciences, in which we find the first approaches to correct geography with regard to the rivers of Central Africa. He recognised three great African rivers: the Senegal,—the Niger itself, which he supposed to flow from west to east,—and the river of Bornu, flowing in an opposite direction, which he erroneously supposed to be Edrisi's 'Nile of the Negroes.' In 1788 we find a Mr. Barnes reporting to the committee of council on Africa that the Niger rises in the eastern extremity of the mountains of Govinea, and discharges itself into a large lake; and that European goods must be transported up the Senegal and down the Niger.-The assoformed in 1788; and in 1797 Mr. Park returned from exploring the course of the Niger, and the territories in its vicinity, during a three years' mission under the society's auspices. In that journey our enterprising countryman traced the confines of the Great Desert, and penetrated, from the Atlantic ocean, more than 1,100 miles eastward into Africa. The French had already extended their travels 500 miles in the same direction; and on the opposite side Mr. Browne had travelled to within about 1,200 miles of Mr. Park's most eastern distance.-In 1797 Frederick Hornemann, a native of Germany, proceeded to Africa under the direction of the association. Attaching himself to a caravan, he travelled from Cairo through Fezzan; and from thence he transmitted various notices respecting the interior of Africa, and in particular affirmed, from the concurring testimony of all travellers with whom he had conversed, that the Niger joins the Nile. Of his farther progress no accounts have reached this country. His name must be added to the list of those who have perished in the cause of African discovery.-In 1805 Mr. Park embarked on a second expedition to Africa, which added little to the geographical knowledge already acquired by his former travels in this country, and cost the intrepid traveller his life. While sailing down the Niger, he was attacked at Yaouri in the kingdom of Houssa, and either killed by the spears of the natives or drowned in the river. His son, a fine young man, visited Africa in 1827, with the design of penetrating into the interior, and completing those discoveries which his father's death had left unaccomplished; but died, by poison it is thought, in the Akimboo country.-Sparmann, Vaillant, Smith, Lichtenstein, Burchell, Campbell, Thompson, Alexander, and various missionaries in that quarter, have communicated interesting information regarding that part of Africa which lies immediately northwards from the boundaries of the Cape district.-Messrs. Salt, Bruce, Burckhardt, Cailliaud, Ruppell, Linant, Palme, and Beke, have thrown considerable light upon the geography of Abessinia and of Eastern Africa.-To the expeditions of Messrs. Denham and Clapperton, and the brothers Lander, we are indebted for much of our knowledge regarding Central Africa.-Captain Tuckey's unfortunate though magnificently planned expedition, has added a little to our knowledge respecting the Congo river.—The French are making us well-acquainted with Algeria; and Egypt and the lower course of the Nile have been well-explored under the auspices of the present energetic ruler of that country. The hydrographical surveys of Smyth, Roussin, Boteler, Belcher, Beechey, Owen, Vidal, Denham, Arlett, and Bérard, of the English and French governments, have produced exceedingly accurate results, and put us in possession of a very correct and nearly complete outline of the coasts of this great continent. Rennel, Barrow, Cooley, M'Queen, and Murray, in our own country, and Ritter, Ukert, Jomard, and Walckenaer, on the continent, have contributed, by their joint labours, to elucidate and arrange many points of African geography. Summary view of the present state of African geography.] The outlines of this continent, then, and a fringe-like space of the coast-lands are pretty well-known to us, especially to the NE and N. From the north of the 10th parallel, and from the 25th to the 40th degree of W long, our notions of African geography are somewhat satisfactory. But what a gap betwixt Darfur and the course of the White Nile; to say nothing of Meroe, of the Galla country, the Abessinian Alps, and the western shore of the Arabian gulf! In South Africa, English missionaries have penetrated to the 22nd and even 20th southern parallel, and we know the general course of the Orange river. The river of Zack upon the left side of the Orange river, and the feeders of the Elephant river more to the S have been visited. But among the questions which yet remain to be solved in this quarter are: the sources of both branches of the Orange river, —the source of the Fish river,—the connexion of the chains of mountains,—the issue of the river of Zack, and of those of Moshowa and Mokatta more to the north,—the extent and direction of the longitudinal chain of the Kamhanni mountains, -the geography of the Lupata chain,-and of the lake Maravi to the eastward. From the 5th southern parallel to the Cape of Good Hope, the lines followed by travellers leave scarcely any interval, but that between the 19th and 26th degree of S lat. except a space in the NE. All the equatorial zone, from the 5th southern parallel to the 5th north, is completely unknown, with the exception of two strips. It is in this vast space that modern geographers find ample room for speculation and theory. Between Senegal and the mountains of Kong, the travels of Adanson, Watt, Winterbottom, Mollien, Laing, and others, have made us pretty well acquainted with the geographical features of the country; but, beyond this, and as far as the confines of Marocco, we know only a narrow border on the continent. It was here that a Houghton, a Roentgen, and a Cochelet, successively fell sacrifices to their daring enterprise and Moorish perfidy.—Browne and Hornemann have the reputation of being the first visitors of Siwah: Hornemann prolonged this line to Fezzan; Lyon went still farther, and discovered the shortest route to Murzuk; Oudney and his fellow-travellers, starting from the latter place, crossed the Sahara, penetrated Haussa, a region of Central Africa inhabited by the Fellatahs, and reached the capital of Bornu, till then placed by geographers 600 m. more to the NE than it should have been. Denham continued his route of discovery 300 m. farther, and on rejoining his companions in Bornu, revealed to them the existence of a vast transversal chain, between the 9th and 10th northern parallels, and of a great central lakethe existence of which had long been doubted-into which the ciation for promoting discoveries in the interior of Africa was | Niger, or at least a river which comes from the quarter of # Timbuktu and Haussa, flows. Clapperton, in a subsequent expedition, traversed the country from Badagry to the Yariba river, crossed the Kong mountains to Boussa on the Niger, returned through Zegzeg, and terminated his career at Sokkatu. Meanwhile a Laing and a Caillié had penetrated to the mysterious city of Timbuktu-the former to meet a tragical death, the latter to give his researches and descriptions to a sceptical world. In 1830, the brothers Lander, one of whom had accompanied Clapperton to Sokkatu, made their way to Boussa, and thence to Yaouri, in which latter country they embarked upon the Niger, and sailing down that river finally reached the gulf of Benin. Mr. Laird of Liverpool prosecuted Lander's discoveries in a commercial expedition to the Upper Niger, in which he ascended its great tributary the Chadda, and reached the city of Fundah, containing 40,000 inhabitants. But after all, " what an immense void," says M. Jomard in his ' Coup d'wil sur les progrés et l'état actuel des découvertes dans l'intérieur de l'Afrique,' "What an immense void is found on a map bounded by these discoveries! What solution is there of the continuity of the 20 or 25 principal lines which African travellers have followed? We have computed the total extent of these lines traversed during 40 years, and have estimated it at 22,000 geographical miles, comprehending therein even the excursions of Poncet in 1698, and those of Bruce in 1768 and 1773. Admitting that each observer has constantly surveyed with his eye an horizon of three leagues in diameter, (and this is a good deal.) we have then but a surface of 28,000 square leagues; and what is this superficies compared with the total of Africa, estimated at 1,400,000 square leagues? Thus Europe scarcely knows a fiftieth part of interior Africa; beyond that, all is confusion and doubt." General Divisions.] It is difficult to classify, and still more to particularize, the different countries and states of which Africa is composed. Its political divisions are ever fluctuating; while its physical features are too imperfectly known to form an accurate basis of arrangement. Ukert, in his laborious and recent account of this imperfectly known division of the globe, has adopted the following arrangement:- I. CAUCASIAN COUNTRIES, VIZ.: I. Egypt. 2. Countries on the Nile, S from Egypt, as far as Abessinia, and extending to the source; of the Nile. 3. Abessinia and the surrounding countries. 4. Countries around the Atlas. 5. The Sahara or Desert, with the Oases. II. NEGRO COUNTRIES, viz.: 1. The countries on the coast, from the White Cape to the Great Fish river, including Senegambia, and Upper and Lower Guinea. 2. Countries of the interior, including Sudan or Nigritia, the districts between Ashantee and Timbuktu, Haussa, and the countries to the E of Sudan as far as Nubia. 3. South Africa, including the Cape colony, the districts of the Hottentots, Caffraria, and the countries stretching along the eastern coast from Delagoa bay to the boundaries of Abessinia. III. THE ISLANDS. The subjoined arrangement of African countries and states, however imperfect, may serve to indicate under what particular heads general information on African geography will be found. I. NORTHERN AFRICA. 1. EGYPT, with MARMARICA and the OASES. 2. Nubia, with Dongola and SENNAAR. I NILOTIC COUNTRIES. the Somaulis, the Da-

NAKIL, and frontier states along the Red Sea. 4. DARFUR and KORDOFAN. 5. The Sahara, extending Darfur, to the Atlantic; the Murghab river. AFRICA. and south from the Bar- II. BARBARY, or MOORbary states to Senegam- ISH AFRICA, OF bia.
MOGHREB, inhabit- 6. TRIPOLI, with BARCA ed by Arabic, Saand FEZZAN, and the

RACENIC, and BEROases to the westward. BER races. 7. Tunis. 8. ALGIERS. William William 9. Marocco, with Fez, Suz, and TAFILET. II. EASTERN AFRICA, Or the region extending from Delagoa bay, by the sea-coast, to the confines of Abessinia. 1 AJAN; 2 ZANGUEBAR; 3 MOZAMBIQUE; 4 Mo-

CARANGA, with SOFALA and INHAMBANE; 5 DELAGOA. III. CENTRAL AFRICA, Sometimes known under the general appellation of Nigritia. 1. NORTHERN NIGRITIA OF SUDAN, being the country to the S of the Sahara, and N of the Kong mountains. 2. Central Nigritia, being the region between the Kong mountains and the gulf of Guinea. 3. Southern Nigritia, extending from the bight of Biafra to Cape Negro, and including Loango, Congo, Angola, and Benguela. IV. WESTERN AFRECA, Extending from the southern skirts of the western Sahara to Cape Negro or Benguela. 1. SENEGAMBIA. 2. GUINEA. V. SOUTHERN AFRICA, Or the region to the south of Cape Negro on the west, and of the Zambese river on the east. 1. Cape Colony, extending from the Orange river southwards, and eastwards to the Great Fish river. 2. Caffraria, Natal, and Interior Districts. VI. THE ISLANDS. Among the numerous African islands not included in any of the foregoing states, are: The MADEIRAS, the Canaries, the Cape Verde Islands, Fernando Po, Ascension, St. Helena, Tristan D'Acunha, the great island of MADAGASCAR, the COMOROS, Bourbon, the Mauritius, and Socotra.

AFRICA, or Afri'ki'YAH (CAPE), a tongue of land on the eastern coast of Tunis, in N lat. 35° 30', E long. 11° 7' [Smyth]; 6 m. to the southward of Ras Dímás, and 5 m. N of Salaktah. It is supposed by D'Anville to have been the site of the Turris Hannibalis. The town of Mehdiyah, once a much frequented port of Barbary, which stands on this point of the coast, sometimes gives its name to the cape, and is sometimes named from it. [Admiralty chart.]
AFRICAN ISLES, a group of islets included in the Mahé cluster of the Seychelles. Lat. 4° 55' S.
AFRICANER, a village in the Namaqua territory of S Africa, 20 m. NE of Naros, and 80 E of Nabis.

AFRIN, a river of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Aleppo, which rises 20 m. SW of Kilis, and flows 3. ABESSINIA, with the | 50 m. in a SSW and W course to Aga Denghis or country of the Gallas, the lake of Antioch, of which it forms one of the chief tributaries. It appears to be identical with the ancient Uprenus. [Chesney's Euphrates and Tigris, vol. i.]
AFSHA'RS, a Turk tribe in Azerdbijan, whose numbers are estimated by M. Brun at 88,000. They speak the Turkee language.
AFSHI'N, or FESHI'N, a town of Persia, in the west from Fezzan and territory of Gharjistan, between Herát and Ghúr, on
AFTA, a small tributary of the Sangarius, flowing | in an easterly course through a narrow and romantic valley betwixt Sabánjah and Gaiwa, in Asia Minor.

AFTAN, a river of Arabia, which rising in the mountains of Yemamah, or flowing NNE from L. Salome, has a course of 350 m. towards El-Bahrein, but is exhausted by evaporation and irrigation before it reaches the Persian gulf.—Chesney's Map of Arabia.
AFT-PUDDLE, a parish in Dorsetshire, 8 m. E by N from Dorchester. Area 4,940 acres. Pop. 507.

AFUERA, one of the Juan Fernandez islands, in the S Pacific, 90 m. to the W of the principal island of that name. It is a craggy rock, with difficult landing, rising to an alt. of 2,300 ft. [King]. The name signifies 'Out,' or Mas afuera, i. e, 'More out,' indicating its greater distance from the American continent. In Ulloa's voyage it is called I. de Afuera.
AFUTLEH, a small village of Asiatic Turkey, in the gov. of Kutahiyah, 31 hours N of Javdéré Hissar. It supplies Constantinople with flints.—Keppel.

AFVA, a small island in the Baltic, belonging to the Aland archipelago, in N lat. 60° 31'. It is on the W side of the Vattuskiftel passage, and N by E of Brandö island. AFVESTADT, AVESTAD, or AVESTA, a town of Sweden, on the Dal, between Grado and Sala. The copper-ore from the Fahlun mine is smelted, and the copper coinage of Sweden minted here. The town is situate close to some cataracts of the Dal. Pop. 800.—Clarke, vol. ix.
AFZIA, a small island in the sea of Marmora to the W of the peninsula of Artaki or Cyziko. It belongs to the sanjak of Bíghá.
AFZULGHUR, or AFZELGERH, a hill-fort and market of Hindostan, on the S frontier of Kumaon, in N lat. 29° 25', E long. 78° 32' [Webb], near the eastern bank of the Ramgunga.
AGA, a lofty round mountain in Brazil, on the route from Minas de Castello to the frontiers of Minas Geraes.
AGA-ACHMET, a v. in the island of Negropont at the foot of Mount Khandhili, 18 m. N by W of Egripos.
AGABJA, a town in the Galla country S of Shoa, betwixt Naréa and Sotto.
AGABLI', or AKABLI', the capital of Tewat, the central oasis of the Sahara, on the line of caravan route from Tripoli, by Ghadames, to Timbuktu.
AGABUZZO, a village of Sicily, in the prov. of Trapani, on the Birgi, 19 m. NNW of Castel Vetrano.
AGADEM, an extensive wady in Central Africa, with several wells of excellent water, 8 days S of Bilma, in the Tibbu country, in N lat. 16° 35'.
AGA' DENGHIS, or WHITE SEA, a lake in Syria, 7 m. N of Antioch, 20 m. in circuit.—Chesney.

AGA'DEZ, or AGHA'DES, a town in the interior of Africa, near the eastern border of the Sahara, on the route from Tripoli to Kashna, in about 16° N lat. Leo represents it as the capital of a Negro state, whose king was of Berber descent, and subject to the king of Timbuktu. Hornemann reports it to be the capital of an independent state called Asben or Azben. It was governed by an independent sheik in 1818. It is probably the Audaghast of the Arabian geographers. From a MS. journal, found in a convent at Tripoli belonging to the Propaganda Fide, quoted in the Quarterly Review [vol. xviii., pp. 374-375] "the ] kingdom of Agadez" appears to have been visited by former.—Ouseley. two reverend fathers, in 1711, who, finding that the objects of the Propaganda could not be prosecuted there, resolved on prosecuting their mission to Cassina [Kashna] at the capital of which they arrived "after a journey of a month with the caravan through the Desert."

AGA-DIP, or AGHA DWIPA, a v. in Bengal, in is manufactured. Pop. in 1826, 200. AGA the Nuddea district, celebrated for an image of Krishna, much venerated by the Hindus, whose offerings are supposed to produce a revenue of £3,000 per ann. to its proprietor. AGA'DI'R, AGADEER, the SANTA CRUZ of the Portuguese, and Da'r Ru'miyyan of the Arabs, a town and port of Marocco. The town is situated on the summit of a hill, 618 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 30° 26', W long. 9° 32' [Arlett], 18 m. from Cape Ghir, from which it bears S 42° E, and 44° m. W of Tarudant. The intervening land forms a deep bay which, according to Jackson, probably affords "the best road for vessels in the empire, being large and well defended on every side from all winds;" but Lieut. Arlett says it is exposed to gales from the W, though, with this exception, he agrees with Jackson in his estimate of it, and adds that it is "quite alive with fish." High barren hills, the western extremity of the main chain of the Atlas, extend from A. to Cape Ghir; and at 9 m. to the E of A. attain an elevation of 4,408 ft. [Arlett]; southwards a low flat country extends for 29 m., and beyond that to Cape Bojador, a distance of 130 m., the shores are altogether inhospitable, being peopled by savage Berber tribes, and skirted by sunken rocks and shallows. About 5 m. N of A., a stream called Wadi Tamaratt discharges itself, at the mouth of a fertile valley, into the sea; and 5 m. to the S is the estuary of the Sús which forms the boundary of the prov. of Sús.—The town of A. originated in a fishing-station of some Portuguese adventurers. Emanuel, king of Portugal, in 1503 converted it into a strongly fortified town. In 1536 it was stormed by the Moors. An English resident describes it, in 1737, as of a square form, and surrounded by walls of about three quarters of a mile in extent, and mounted with artillery. At that time it carried on considerable commerce, exporting copper procured from mines near Tafilet, wax, almonds, gums, ivory, gold dust, saltpetre, leather, and ostrich-feathers; and importing iron, muskets, hides, and European merchandise. [Pinkerton's Collection, vol. xv., p. 715.] Sidi Mohammed dismantled it in 1774, and transferred its pop. to his new city of Mogador. Its walls are in ruins; but the inhabitants, who are about 300 in number, carry on some fishing-trade with Mogador, which is 76 m. distant, and with the interior. In the reign of Muley Ismail, A. was the centre of an extensive commerce with the tribes of the interior; and security of property and good government only are wanting to make it the emporium of Marocco and once more the Bab Sudan, or 'Gate of Sudan.'—Lempriere's Tour .- Jackson's Marocco. - Lieut. Arlett in Geog. Journ., vol. vi.
AGAFONOW, a river of Siberia rising in an unexplored territory, and flowing into the sea, by a course to the NE, about 70°36' N lat., and 159° 30' E long.

AGA'JIK, or AKA'JIK, a v. of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, on the road from Tabriz to Erzrum. About 3 m. NW of this v. is a town marking the boundary line between Persia and Turkey in this quarter.

AGA-KEMAL-BALA, or AAKEMAL-BALA, a caravanserai in a barren undulating tract of the district of Cashan, 48 m. N of Ispahan, and 15 m. from Kuhrud. -There is another caravanseral of the same name, but distinguished by the adjunct Paiun [i. e. 'the Lower; Bala signifying 'the Upper,' 13 m. S of the

AGALEGA, an island of Africa, N part, in 10° 214' S lat., E long. 56° 38'; 560 m. NNW from the Mauritius. It is 15 m. long from NW to SE, and 11 m. broad. A channel, fordable at low water, and about 1,000 yards in width, divides it into two parts. Its chief productions are cocoa-nut trees, from which oil
AGA-LIMAN, or AGHALIMAN [i.e. 'Port Agha'], a small sheltered bay on the coast of Asiatic Turkey, in the sanjak of Itchil, 9 m. SE of Selefkeh, the ancient Seleucia, of which it formed the harbour.
AGAMANA, a v. in the pashalik of Baghdad, on the r. bank of the Euphrates, 52 m. W of Anna.
AGAME', a district of Abessinia, to the E of Tigré, and NW of the great salt plain. It is an elevated table-land, fertile and well-cultivated. Its chief town is Genata.
AGAMENTICUS, a noted hill in the NW part of York co., in the state of Maine, U.S. It is 4 m. from the sea, and has an alt. of 673 ft., 43° 13' N, 70° 41' W.
AGA'MNA, a monastery in Abessinia, between Yaush and Móta, near the head of the Bora river.

AGA'NA (S. YGNACIO DE), the capital of Guajan, the principal island of the Ladrones. They reckon 570 houses in A., only 50 of which are built of stone. The rest are mere huts of two rooms, framed of the mid-ribs of the cocoa-tree, and thatched with leaves, standing in a little garden of tobacco plants bordered by sago trees. They are however regularly placed, and form tolerably wide streets. Except those of stone, all are built upon piles 4 ft high. [Arago.] A. is situated 4 m. from the mouth of a small river on the NW coast, and has only an open roadstead; but there is a good harbour 10 m. to the S, called Calderona de Apura. Pop. in 1816, 3,118.
AGANIS, a district on the E coast of Ceylon, a little to the N of the Naveloorar river.
AGARDAM, or AGHARDAM (CAPE), a headland on the Tungusi coast of Siberia, 45 m. ENE of the mouth of the Iana river, 71° 50' N, 137° 10' E.

AGA'RIB, or AGRIB (MOUNT), or JEBEL GHA'RIB, the highest peak in the desert between the Nile and the Red sea, attaining an elevation of about 6,000 ft. [Wilkinson], 10,000 ft. [Moresby], and seen, at the distance of 100 m., on entering the gulf of Suez. It is in N lat. 28° 7', E long. 32° 52'. The more correct orthography, perhaps, is Akrab.
AGASONOWKA, a river of Siberia, which has its mouth in about 69° 40' N lat., and 163° 20' E long.

AGATA (SANT'), a small town in the States-of-the- Church, in the deleg. of Urbino and Pesaro, 10 m. WSW of San Marino, on a small stream which flows into the Savio.—Also a v. of Naples in the Capitanata, 8 m. SSE of Bovino.

AGATA (SANT'), a mountain in the island of Minorca, the second in rank after the central elevation of Monte Toro. It lies to the NW of Mercadal, and is surrounded by a number of inferior wooded eminences. On the summit are some remains of fortifications, and two immense cisterns, the cubical contents of which, Armstrong says, amount to 2,190,384 Paris pints.—St. Sauveur.

AGATA DE' GOTI (SANT'), a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavoro, 15 m. E by S of Capua. Pop. 2,700. The town is placed in a picturesque situation upon the edge of a precipice, at the N entrance to a narrow valley traversed by a rivulet called the Isclero, which, entering the defile near Moiano, passes betwixt St. A. and Mount Taburnus, on the opposite side of the ravine, and falls into the Vulturnus, 3 m. to the NW. Cluverius has, with much probability, placed the Furca Caudina in this ravine or defile. Other antiquaries identify the Caudine Forks with the valley to the westward, nearly at right angles to it, between Arionzo and Arpaia, across the eastern entrance of which the Isclero runs.—See Craven's Tour.
AGATA NUOVA (SANT'), a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra Prima, 8 m. ESE of Reggio. Pop. 1,200.—S. A. Vecchia is a v. a little to the E of the new town.
AGATCH. See KIZIL-AGATCH. AGE
AGATHA, a small town on the N coast of the island of Cyprus, 20 m. NNW of Famagosta.

AGATHENBURG, a v. and castle in the duchy of Bremen, 3 m. SE of Stade. AGATHO, AGATTON, GATTO, or GATO, a town of Western Africa, near the head of a deep creek on the N side of the Benin river, which opens 3 leagues from the entrance of the river. The road from this town to Benin passes through a level swampy country in a direction NE by N. Belzoni, the celebrated traveller, died at this place on the 3d of Dec. 1823, when about to push his way to the Niger from this point.

AGATHONISI, or GADARONISI, a small island in the Greek archipelago, off the coast of Natolia, and included in the sanjak of Sighlah; in 37° 28' N lat., 27° 2' E long.; between Cape Colonna in Samos, and Cape Arbora in Natolia.
AGATTU, one of the Aleutian islands, in the western group, 20 m. E of Attu. It has a lofty central peak. Lat. 52° 43' N, long. 173° 37' E.

AGATTU-YANOO, a large town of Eastern Africa, in the territory of the Moluas, in S lat. 1° 30', which Douville describes as the handsomest Negro town he had seen. It is built upon an island formed by dividing branches of a river called Agattu, which here runs towards the east. Pop. 15,000. It is sometimes known by the name Tandi-a-voua.
AGAWAM, a v. of Massachusetts, U.S., in Hampden county, on the west side of the Connecticut river, 98 m. W of Boston.
AGAY, a small fishing v. of France, on the coast of the Mediterranean, in the dep. of Var, 41 m. W of Frejus.
AGDASS. See AGADEZ.

AGDE, a maritime town of France, in the dep. of Hérault, in Languedoc, 11 m. E of Beziers, on the r. bank of the Hérault. It is situated in a fertile district; and is a place of much commercial activity, forming one of the entrepots between the S and W of France, and Italy and Spain. Pop. of commune and town 8,202. The town is built of a dark-coloured basalt, and hence is sometimes called 'Ville Noire.' It has a very old cathedral. The Canal du Midi passes near the town. The port of A., at the mouth of the Herault, and to the westward of Cape Agde, is in N lat. 43° 17′, E long. 3° 27′; and a light is exhibited from the Mont d'Agde, 3\frac{1}{4} m. N 66\frac{1}{2}\circ E true from the mouth of the river, at an elevation of 413 ft. In 1837, 74,648 tons of shipping entered and left the port of A. In 1844, 367 vessels entered the port; and 359 sailed from it. The total tonnage inwards was 31,609 tons; outwards 32,751.—A. was the Agatha of Gallia Narbonensis. In 506, Alaric convoked a council at this place. In 1634 a series of works were commenced, under the auspices of Richelieu, with the view of improving the navigation of the Hérault and forming a port to this town; but although large sums of money have been expended on these, the port is by no means a good one, and is chiefly used by the smaller class of coasting-vessels.—Jourdan, Hist. de la ville d'Agde.
AGDENAES, a promontory on the coast of Norway, in the gulf of Drontheim; in N lat. 63° 38', E long. 9° 43'.

AGEN, a town of France, formerly the capital of the Agenois in Guienne, now the chief town of the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne. It is situated on the r. bank of the Garonne, 16 m. ENE of Nérae, and 63 SE of Bordeaux; in N lat. 44° 12′, W long. 0° 26′. Pop. in 1821, 10,750; in 1841, 14,161. Its position on a fine navigable river, between Toulouse and Bordeaux, has rendered it a place of considerable traffic and some manufactures, of which the most extensive is that of sail-cloth. It is the seat of a bishop, a suffragan of Bordeaux, and has a public library of 15,000 vols. It is a place of great antiquity. Ptolemy mentions it as being the capital of the Nitiobriges. It was successively taken and pillaged by the Visigoths, the Huns, the Vandals, and the Normans; and passed by turns into the hands of the kings of France, the dukes of Aquitaine, the kings of England, and the counts of Toulouse. It espoused the cause of the League in 1584. Few cities have produced a greater number of illustrious men. A. was the birth-place of Sulpicius Severus, of Joseph Scaliger, of Bernard Palissy, of Lacépède, Lamouroux, Bory de Saint- Vincent, and the Provençal poet Jasmin.—[Brecy, Esquisses historiques, &c.-Patria.]-The arrondissement of A. comprehends the 8 cantons of Agen, Astafort, Beauville, La Plume, Port Sainte-Marie, Prayssas, Puymirol, and La Roque-Timbaut. Area, 100,631 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 84,383.
AGENAGATAMINGA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which, rising in about 6° 10' S lat., 60° W long., flows in a NNW direction into the Madera.

AGENOIS, an ancient division of French Guienne, bounded on the N by Perigord, on the E by Quercy, on the S by Garonne, and on the W by Bazadois; and comprehending the modern dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, Dordogne, Tarn-et-Goronne, and Gironde.
AGER, a small town of Spain, in the prov. of Catalonia, 12 m. N by E of Balaguer.—Also a river of Austria, which joins the Traun near Lambach.
AGERKOUF. See AKKERKUF.
AGEROE, or AGERSOE, an island of Denmark, in the Great Belt, 7 m. S of Corsoer in Sieland, and 2 m. NE by N of Omoe island.
AGEROLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato Citra, and cant. of Amalfi, 12 m. WSW of Salerno. It is pleasantly situated on a hill. Pop. 2,200.
AGES, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Burgos, 9 m. E of Burgos, in the mountains of Oca.
AGGA. See AGUNA.

AGGER-CHANNEL, an opening recently formed by an irruption of the sea, on the W coast of Jutland, between 56° 42' and 56° 43' N lat., by which small vessels may now pass, through the lakes of Jutland, from the North sea into the Cattegat. The depth of the western entrance varies from 5 to 7 ft. It receives its name from the small fishing-port of Agger at its entrance.

AGGERHUUS, one of the five great divisions or dioceses of Norway, now more generally called, from the capital, Christiania. It is bounded on the N by the prov. of Drontheim; on the E by Sweden; on the S by the arm of the North sea called the Skagerrack; and on the W by the provinces of Christiansand and Bergen. It lies between the parallels of 58° 45' and 62° 40' N.; and, according to Forsell, has a superficies of nearly 31,000 sq. m. It is divided into five bailiwicks, and two counties: viz.- Population. Area. Macculloch. Forsell. Other admeasurements carry the superficial area of this region still higher. Thus the writer of the article

NORWAY, in the Encyclopædia Metropolitana, assigns 31,500 sq. m. to it; while in the article Christiania, in the Penny Cyclopædia, its superficies is estimated at 35,820 sq. m., or nearly 7,000 sq. m. more than the area of Scotland .- A. is a wild romantic region, covered with mountains and lakes, and thickly intersected with rivers. The Dovrefield and the Langfield ranges AGH of snowy mountains divide it, on the W and N, from Bergen and Drontheim. These ranges have an average breadth of 60 m.; and in the northern part of the prov. send numerous offsets across the country towards the frontiers of Sweden. The eastern side of the prov. is intersected throughout by the river Glommen, the largest river of Norway, along the banks of which the most level and fertile districts occur. The coast along the Skager-rack is bold, deeply indented, and fringed with numerous rocky islets.—The Drammen-elf, the Lougen-elf, the Louven-elf, and the Skeen-elf, are all large rivers in this district.—The principal lakes are the Fämundsöe in the NE, on the Swedish boundary; the Miösen towards the centre; and the Teroen or Tyrifiord, NW of Christiania.- The climate is severe in winter, especially on the eastern declivity of the Dovrefield; but the mean temperature of Christiania is 42°. The heat of summer is often excessive.-Extensive forests of oak, beech, fir, and pine clothe the lower declivities of the mountains. - The mineral productions are iron, copper, alum, cobalt, and silver. The produce of the latter-which is wrought at Kongsberg-is said to be increasing.—The agricultural produce of the district consists of wheat, rye, barley, oats, pease, and potatoes. Oats is the most extensively cultivated grain; and next to it, barley; wheat is chiefly raised in Smaalehnen, and Buskerud.—The principal towns are Christiania on the bay of that name, with a pop. of 27,000; Drammen, on the Drammen-fiord, 7,000; Laurvig, at the mouth of the Louven-elf, 3,600; Frederickstad, at the mouth of the Glommen, 3,000; Frederickshald at the mouth of the Tistedal, 5,300; Tonsberg, 2,200; and Kongsberg, 4,400. All these towns, with the exception of the last, are on or near the coast.-The bailiwick of A. or Christiania has an area, as above indicated, of about 2,000 sq. m., and is divided into 42 parishes. It includes the towns of Christiania, Drammen, Kongsberg, and Dröbak. Pop. in 1801, 56,899; in 1821, 64,871; in 1835, 94,832, of whom 69,827 resided in the rural districts, and 25,005 in townships. Of the pop. of 1835, 4,152 were returned as udalmen or proprietors, and 1,523 as farmers; 1,282 as artisans; 2,676 as labourers; and 2,488 as paupers.
AGGERNAES, a town of Denmark, on the N coast of the isle of Funen, 14 m. N of Odensee.
AGGIUS, a town in the island of Sardinia, 4 m. NW of Tempio. Pop. 2,450. Its inhabitants were considered little better than brigands in 1746.

AGGSBACH (GREAT and LITTLE), two villages in the archd. of Austria, in the circle of Manhartsberg, on opposite sides of the Danube, 12 m. WNW. of Saint Pölten.—Above Little Aggsbach, which is on the l. side of the river, rises a high conical rock whereon is perched the castle of Aggstein, celebrated in early German history.

AGHA, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Esv. of A. is 5 m. SE of Borris-in-Ossory. It is a poor but a very ancient place, having been the site of a Culdee establishment in the 6th cent. The ruins of a Dominican abbey, founded here in 1250, still exist. The pop. is chiefly Roman Catholic.
AGHABOG, a p. of Ireland, in co. Monaghan, 5 m. ESE of Clones. Area 11,543 acres. Pop. 7,530, of whom about two-thirds are Roman Catholics.
AGHABOLOGUE, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork. Area 18,733 acres. Pop. 6,001, of whom five-sixths are Catholics.
AGHACREW, or AUGHACREW, a p. of Ireland, in co. Tipperary. Area 1,231 acres. Pop. 409, all Catholics.
AGHACROSS, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork, on the skirts of the Galtee mountains. Area 355 acres. Pop. 104.
AGHADA, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork, extending along the eastern shore of Cork harbour. Area 2,459 acres. Pop. 2,835, nearly all Catholics.
AGHADE, a p. of Ireland, in co. Carlow. Area 1,698 acres. Pop. 521.
AGHADEEP. See AGADIP.
AGHADEM. See AGADEM.

AGHADERG, or AGHADERRICK, a p. of Ireland, in co. Down. Area 13,920 acres. Pop. 9,240, of whom the majority are Protestants. It contains the town of Loughbrickland, and the villages of Scarva and Poyntzpass.
AGHADES. See AGADEZ.

AGHADOE, a p. of Ireland, in co. Kerry. Area 19,888 acres. Pop. 4,897. The scenery of this p., which includes the lower lake of Killarney, is very romantic; and it contains some ancient ecclesiastical ruins. The ancient diocese of A. has long been merged in that of Ardfert.
AGHADOWN, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork, stretching along the river Ilen, and the W shore of Baltimore harbour, and including several small islands. Area 8,952 acres. Pop. 5,757, chiefly Catholics.
AGHADOWY, or AGHADWEY, a p. of Ireland, in co. Londonderry. Area 16,346 acres. Pop. 7,884, chiefly Presbyterians. The v. of Caheny is in this parish.

AGHAGALLEN, a p. of Ireland, in co. Antrim, on the E of Lough Neagh. Area 5,469 acres. Pop. 3,862. AGHAT-DENIZI'-i. e. 'The Ocean of Trees'a name given by the Turks to the forests which clothe the northern declivity of the mountain-chain stretching between the Sangarius and the Bosphorus, to the N and NE of Ismíd.
AGHA'KANEIRA', a district of the Sahara, densely peopled by Tuaricks, 54 days W from Ghát.
AGHA'L (WADI), a valley in Fezzan, 40 m. S of the Jebal-el-Súda or Black mountains.
AGHALEE, a p. of Ireland, in co. Antrim. Area 2,499 acres. Pop. 1,450. The surface has a westerly dip toward Lough Neagh.
AGHA'L-GORI, a town in the Russian prov. of Georgia, situated in a narrow fertile valley, whence a road leads up the Caucasus.
AGHA-LIMAN. See Aga-Liman.

AGHALURCHER, a p. of Ireland, in co. Tyrone. Area 48,017 acres, of which 3,130 consist of some small lakes, and a portion of Upper Lough Erne, which extends along the western border. Pop. 16,740, under one-half of whom are Catholics. It comprises the post-town of Lisnaskea, the market-town of Maguire's Bridge, and several villages.

AGHA'LUSU'N, or AGLASUN, a v. and kadilik of Natolia, in the sanjak of Hamid, 8 m. SW of Ispartah. In the neighbourhood are extensive ruins, the remains, it seems probable, of the ancient Sagalassus; and a small stream bearing the name of the v., and flowing towards Adalia, may be the ancient Cestrus.—Arundell.
AGHAMORE, or AUGHAMORE, a p. of Ireland, in co. Mayo. Area 22,280 acres. Pop. 7,675, all Catholics.
AGHANCON, or AGHINCON, a p. of Ireland, in King's co. Area 5,544 acres. Pop. 1,310, chiefly Catholics.

AGHANLOO, a p. of Ireland, in co. Londonderry, on the SE shores of the estuary of the Foyle. Area 8,251 acres. Pop. 1,841, of whom two-thirds are Presbyterians. In this p. is Benyevenagh, which I tan, in Turkish Armenia. Its bishop has for many AGH soars up from the edge of Lough Foyle to an alt. of 1,280 ft.
AGHA-RUGAB, a town of Afghanistan, in Ghorat, on the SE slope of the Paropamisus.
AGHARU'N, an Armenian v. in Kurdistán, beautifully situated in a gorge of the Darkush-Tagh, above the v. of Nerjki.-Brant.

AGHAVALLAH, a p. of Ireland, in co. Kerry. Area 16,743 acres. Pop. 6,606, chiefly Catholics. The surface declines northward to the Shannon. It contains the town of Ballylongford, and the island of Carrigafoyle.
AGHER, a p. of Ireland, in co. Meath, part of an elevated tract between the Boyne and the Liffey rivers. Area 2,063 acres. Pop. 386, chiefly Catholics.
AGHERN, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork, on the Bride river. Area 3,489 acres. Pop. 1,198, chiefly Catholics.
AGHERTON, or Ballyaghron, a p. of Ireland, in co. Londonderry, on the E of the river Bann. Area 3,896 acres. Pop. 2,318, chiefly Presbyterians. It contains the v. of Port-Stewart.
AGHIART, a p. of Ireland, in co. Galway. See BALLINAKILTY.
AGHMA'T, a branch of the Tensift, in Marocco, rising near a v. of the same name on the southern slope of Mount Miltsin, and flowing NW to the Tensift, which it joins 25 m. below Marocco.
AGHNAMEADLE, a p. of Ireland, on the northern border of co. Tipperary. Area 10,322 acres. Pop. 3,893, chiefly Catholics. It contains the small town of Toomavara.
AGHNAMEEN, an immense morass or bog on the l. bank of the rivers Flesk and Maine, 9 m. N of Killarney, in Ireland. Area 87,240 acres.
AGHNISH, or Augnish (Point), a narrow headland on the N side of Galway bay, co. Galway, Ireland. It forms the eastern point of Burrin harbour, with an entrance 200 yards wide, and 2½ fath. deep.
AGHOLD, a p. of Ireland, in co. Wicklow. Area 8,140 acres. Pop. 2,744, chiefly Catholics. The v. of A. is 44 m. ESE of Tullow.

AGHOR, a river of Beluchistan, which, rising in Lus, runs along the SE boundary of the prov. of Mekran, and flows into the Indian ocean, in N lat. 25° 19', E long. 65° 26'. Hart found it 60 yards wide, and 11 ft. deep, at a point 6 m. from its mouth in the month of January; but its volume of water must be greatly enlarged during the melting of the snows, if its source is far to the N. Near the r. bank, 30 m. from its mouth, is the cave of Hinglai, a celebrated place of Hindu pilgrimage. The A. is called HINGOL in the upper part of its course.
AGHOSTOS, or Aughistos, a town of European Turkey, in the sanjak of Salonik, on the l. bank of a stream flowing into Lake Takinos. Lat. 41° N.
AGHOURE, or Freshford, a p. of Ireland, in co. Kilkenny. Area 2,171 acres. Pop. 2,650. This p. forms part of the fine vale of the Nore river, and contains the town of Freshford.

AGHRIM, or AUGHRIM, a p. of Ireland, in co. Roscommon, bounded on the NE by the Shannon. Area 8,255 acres. Pop. 4,469.—Also a p. in co. Galway. Area 7,251 acres. Pop. 2,127. The v. of A. in this p. marks the scene of the last great battle between the forces of James II. and those of William III., fought on 12th July, 1691.

AGHRIS (Point), a promontory on the N coast of co. Sligo, Ireland; 11 m. W of Sligo, in N lat. 54° 17', W long. 9° 22'. Its face is a precipitous limestone cliff, on the summit of which are some remains of ancient fortifications.
AGHTAMA'R, or IGTAMAR, an Armenian monastery on an island in lake Van, to the W of Vascenturies assumed the title and exercised the functions of Catholicos.
AGHVEH, or AGHREH, a town of Natolia, in the sanjak of Kójahili, on a small stream running into the Black sea, in N lat. 41° 10', E long. 29° 52'.
AGHVERA'N, a Kurd v. in the beglik of Kinis, in Kurdistán, 3½ hours from Koïli, at an elevation of 6,200 ft. above sea-level.—Brant.
AGHYA'Z, a large v. in Kurdistán, between Eipler and Kőilí.
AGIA-ANNA (CAPE), a point of land on the NE coast of the island of Negropont; in N lat. 38° 53', E long. 23° 27'.
AGIA-EUPHEMIA, av. 3 hours N of Galixidi in Phocis; near it are the ruins of a walled city, probably the ancient Ipnos.
AGIAKI, a v. of Greece, on the gulf of Lepanto, S of Livadia.
AGIA-MAMA, a v. of Turkey, in the sanjak of Selanik, at the head of the gulf of Cassandra, probably near the site of the ancient Olynthos.
AGIA-MONI, a v. of Thessaly, to the SW of Trikala, near the site of the ancient Metropolis.
AGID, or Egidy (St.), a v. of Lower Austria, in the Wienerwald circle, on a branch of the Trasen, 25 m. S of St. Pölten. It is celebrated for its manufacture of guns, swords, and steel instruments.
AGIKOI, a town of Bulgaria, NE of Paravadia.
AGILOTS, a tribe of Indians inhabiting some districts on the Rio de la Plata.

AGINCOURT, or Azincourt, a v. of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, 15 m. WNW of Saint-Pol, on the l. of the road from Abbeville to St. Omer. Pop. 500, A. is famous as marking the site of the battle in which Henry V. of England, with 812 men-at-arms, and 3,071 archers, on the 25th of October, 1415, defeated a French army vastly more numerous than his own. Eager to conquer France, and encouraged by the disturbed condition of the country, the English monarch landed at Harfleur, took that place after a siege of 36 days, and set out, through Picardy, to Calais. With a powerful force, the dauphin advanced against him. The numerical superiority of the French was such, that they refused the proffered aid of the duke of Burgundy, and of the city of Paris. Henry retreated upon the Somme, and gained the passage from Abbeville to St. Quentin, through the inattention of the enemy: the French, however, succeeded in throwing themselves between the English and Calais. Henry's army was ranged in order of battle between two hills, with the archers on the wings. The French, commanded by the constable D'Albert, numbered, it has been said, 100,000, of whom 8,000 were men-at-arms. They arranged themselves in three lines, with the men-at-arms, of whom 2,000 were mounted, in front. The English first put themselves in motion. The French horse hastened to meet them, but were received with such a shower of arrows by the archers, that they fell back upon the first line and threw it into confusion; whereupon the light-armed English archers seized their clubs and battle-axes, and broke into the ranks of the knights-on-foot, who were encumbered by their heavy coats of mail and the closeness of their array. The first French line gave way; the second could not sustain the charge of the victors; and the whole army was soon entirely scattered. Henry fearing that the French would rally and renew the battle, and alarmed by the report that a party of peasants were plundering his baggage, ordered all the prisoners to be massacred. The victorious army, however, took 14,000 prisoners more; and 10,000 Frenchmen, amongst whom were the constable, three dukes, five counts, and ninety barons, fell in the field. Five princes, among whom were the dukes of Orleans and Bourbon, were taken prisoners. The English lost 1,600 men. The duke of York, Henry's uncle, was killed by the duke d'Alençon, who, pressing towards the king, had already dashed the crown from his head, and was lifting his hand for a more effectual blow, when the king's attendants surrounded him, and he fell covered with wounds. After the battle, Henry continued his march to Calais; and thence sailed for England.
AGIO-ELIAS, an islet off the coast of Thessaly, between Sciathi and Kelidromi.
AGIO-GIORGIO (CAPE), or CAPE St. GEORGE, the SE extremity of Tricala or Thessaly, in N lat. 39° 12′, E long. 23° 23′. It is the ancient Magnesian promontory.
AGIOLOI-BASSARDSECK. See HADJI-OGLUBAZARJIK. AGN
AGIO-MYRO, a v. of Crete, SE of Mount Strombolo. It is celebrated for its excellent wine.
AGION-OROS. See ATHOS.
AGIO-RUMELI, a small v. on the S coast of Crete.

AGIOS-GEORGIOS, a large and populous v. of the Morea, in the dep. of Corinthia, 12 m. from Argos, in the vale of the Asopus. It is surrounded by vineyards, and celebrated for its excellent red wine. - Gell.
